First Author: Adrian Gerard Williams, PhD
Order of Authors: Adrian Gerard Williams, PhD; Ilkka Leinonen, PhD

Abstract: There is an increased interest in the use of poultry litter as fuel by the relevant industries. Hence the environmental impacts of using turkey litter as fuel to generate electricity instead of using litter as fertilizer were systematically analysed for the first time. For this purpose, a systems modelling-based Life Cycle Assessment approach was used, with data obtained directly from the UK turkey industry. Impacts were calculated per 1000 kg turkey live weight produced at the farm gate (functional unit) : The avoided burdens method was used to quantify the effects of the alternative litter use. Differences in the environmental impacts between the two litter use scenarios resulted from the combined effect of the following sub-processes: the loss of nitrogen as a crop fertiliser, the transport for collecting litter and distributing the ash as a phosphorus and potassium fertiliser, displacement of electricity generation by a combined cycle gas turbine, specific trace gas emissions from combustion and the loss of soil carbon from the reduced organic matter supply to arable soils. The results showed that there are substantial environmental benefits from using turkey litter as a fuel to generate electricity rather than using it directly as a fertiliser with reductions in burdens of cumulative primary energy demand (14%), eutrophication potential (55%) and acidification potential (70%) The reduction in acidification and eutrophication potentials were mainly associated with reduced ammonia emissions from the storage and land spreading of the litter. Reductions in greenhouse gas emissions were small (3%) because losses of soil carbon as a result of not applying litter to land partially compensated the benefits of reduced fossil energy use. Small increases in nitrogen oxides, volatile organic carbon, particulate matter below 10 um aerodynamic diameter and dioxin emissions were found, although only nitrogen oxides were especially linked to combustion. Despite its potential benefits, stringent management, monitoring and regulation of biomass fuelled power production is still needed, given the potential hazards of local high emissions. Although turkey litter was analysed in this study, similar results can be expected for broiler litter use as a fuel, as long as geographical conditions are similar.

First Author: Adrian Gerard Williams, PhD
Order of Authors: Adrian Gerard Williams, PhD; Ilkka Leinonen, PhD

Abstract: There is an increased interest in the use of poultry litter as fuel by the relevant industries. Hence the environmental impacts of using turkey litter as fuel to generate electricity instead of using litter as fertilizer were systematically analysed for the first time. For this purpose, a systems modelling-based Life Cycle Assessment approach was used, with data obtained directly from the UK turkey industry. Impacts were calculated per 1000 kg turkey live weight produced at the farm gate (functional unit) : The avoided burdens method was used to quantify the effects of the alternative litter use. Differences in the environmental impacts between the two litter use scenarios resulted from the combined effect of the following sub-processes: the loss of nitrogen as a crop fertiliser, the transport for collecting litter and distributing the ash as a phosphorus and potassium fertiliser, displacement of electricity generation by a combined cycle gas turbine, specific trace gas emissions from combustion and the loss of soil carbon from the reduced organic matter supply to arable soils. The results showed that there are substantial environmental benefits from using turkey litter as a fuel to generate electricity rather than using it directly as a fertiliser with reductions in burdens of cumulative primary energy demand (14%), eutrophication potential (55%) and acidification potential (70%) The reduction in acidification and eutrophication potentials were mainly associated with reduced ammonia emissions from the storage and land spreading of the litter. Reductions in greenhouse gas emissions were small (3%) because losses of soil carbon as a result of not applying litter to land partially compensated the benefits of reduced fossil energy use. Small increases in nitrogen oxides, volatile organic carbon, particulate matter below 10 um aerodynamic diameter and dioxin emissions were found, although only nitrogen oxides were especially linked to combustion. Despite its potential benefits, stringent management, monitoring and regulation of biomass fuelled power production is still needed, given the potential hazards of local high emissions. Although turkey litter was analysed in this study, similar results can be expected for broiler litter use as a fuel, as long as geographical conditions are similar.

1|| 2|39|"Poultry litter in the UK (mainly coming from broiler, egg and turkey production) was normally managed only as a fertiliser and soil conditioner until the late 1990s. The high plant nutrient" 3 4|40|"Poultry litter in the UK (mainly coming from broiler, egg and turkey production) was normally managed only as a fertiliser and soil conditioner until the late 1990s. The high plant nutrient" 5|41|nitrogen( phosphorus (P) and potassium (K) concentrations make it more valuable than other 6|42|(semi-) solid manures (Defra, 2010). The nutrient contents of turkey and broiler litter are very similar 7 43|7 43|and Defra (2010) does not differentiate between their compositions. Turkey and broiler litter 8 44|8 44|includes some bedding, usually wood shavings or chopped straw. Losses of N during litter storage 10 9 45|10 9 45|may occur, which can lead to substantial emissions of ammonia (NH3), and a mixture of di-nitrogen 11 46|11 46|(N2), nitrogen oxide and dioxide (NOx) and nitrous oxide (N20). These change the actual amount of N 12 47|12 47|available when applied to land and hence the fertiliser value. Here, we use turkey litter as an 13 48|13 48|example of the consequences of using the litter as fuel, instead of applying it to land. Similar 14 49|14 49|arguments apply to the use of broiler litter as fuel, but we had better access to key aspects of turkey 16 15 50|16 15 50|litter management activity data and hence report this rather than broiler litter. 17 51|17 51|UK turkey annual production increased from the 1970s to a maximum of 40 M birds in 1995, steadily 18 52|18 52|fell to about 15 M in 2007, and then remained relatively constant (Defra 2014). The production of 20 19 53|20 19 53|turkey litter from 17.5 M slaughterings in 2013 was 0.9 Mt, which contained 21 kt N, 7.7 kt P (or 21 54|21 54|17.6 kt phosphorus pentoxide [P2O5]) and 13 kt K (or 15.7 kt potassium oxide [K2O]), using the 22 55|22 55|composition data from Defra (2010). There are poultry (of all species) across most of the country, 23 56|23 56|with higher concentrations in some parts, e.g. East Anglia, which also supports a large fraction of UK 24 57|24 57|turkey production (Figure 1). This has contributed to pressures in the nitrate vulnerable zones (NVZ) 25 58|25 58|that were introduced as implementation of the 1991 EU nitrates directive, which became more 27 26 59|27 26 59|stringent since being introduced. 28 60|28 60|Two important features affect the application of manure as a fertiliser: an upper limit on N applied 30 29 61|30 29 61|per ha and the exclusion of applications in late autumn and early winter (Defra, 2013). With high 31 62|31 62|concentrations of poultry in some areas, finding an alternative to land application consequently 32 63|32 63|became attractive to farmers. This was enhanced by the potential of using litter as a fuel, which 33 64|33 64|added to the UK's use of renewable biomass for generating electricity. The use as fuel started in 34 65|34 65|1992 and consumed about 13% of UK poultry litter, with the proportion increasing to a current value 36 35 66|36 35 66|of about 35% (data used in the UK ammonia inventory, T.H. Misselbrook, Pers. Comm.). Poultry litter 37 67|37 67|has been used in the Netherlands for electricity generation since 2008 (BMC Moerdijk (2008), Lynch 38 68|38 68|et al. (2013) and Quiroga et al. (2010) and investigated the properties of poultry litter as fuel by 39 69|39 69|direct combustion. Jia and Anthony (2011) examined co-combustion of poultry litter and coal. More 40 70|40 70|work seems to have addressed the performance of gasification or pyrolysis of poultry litter (e.g. Di 41 71|41 71|Gregorio et al. (2014), Font-Palma (2012), Huang et al. (2015) and Striúgas et al. (2014). These 43 42 72|43 42 72|studies focussed on the technological performance of processes. 44 73|44 73|Sandars et al. (2003) were the first authors to apply life cycle assessment (LCA) to manure 46 45 74|46 45 74|management in the UK, in this case, land application by different technologies. Reijnders and 47 75|47 75|Huijbregts (2005) applied LCA to burning animal wastes in the European Union, including litter and 48 76|48 76|other materials. They found that the greenhouse gas emissions of burning animal waste were very 49 77|49 77|sensitive to the allocation approach chosen. They did not address indirect effects, such as changes in 50 78|50 78|fertiliser use (and hence production) which could considerably change the emissions of greenhouse 51 79|51 79|gases. They considered that such quantification should be the subject of further research. Billen et 53 52 80|53 52 80|al. (2015) compared poultry litter being applied to land as a fertiliser with combustion for electricity 54 81|54 81|generation, in the Netherlands using LCA. Main impacts (and benefits for electricity production) 55 82|55 82|related mainly to reduced greenhouse gas from fossil fuel use and emissions of ammonia, nitrous 56 83|56 83|oxide. Further benefits arose from the enhanced ability to export P and K fertiliser in ash away from 58 57 84|58 57 84|areas that were potentially over-supplied. 59 85|59 85| 60|60| 61|61| 62|62| 63|63| 64|64|Page 3 of 20 65|65|

1|| 2|39|"Poultry litter in the UK (mainly coming from broiler, egg and turkey production) was normally managed only as a fertiliser and soil conditioner until the late 1990s. The high plant nutrient" 3 4|40|"Poultry litter in the UK (mainly coming from broiler, egg and turkey production) was normally managed only as a fertiliser and soil conditioner until the late 1990s. The high plant nutrient" 5|41|nitrogen( phosphorus (P) and potassium (K) concentrations make it more valuable than other 6|42|(semi-) solid manures (Defra, 2010). The nutrient contents of turkey and broiler litter are very similar 7 43|7 43|and Defra (2010) does not differentiate between their compositions. Turkey and broiler litter 8 44|8 44|includes some bedding, usually wood shavings or chopped straw. Losses of N during litter storage 10 9 45|10 9 45|may occur, which can lead to substantial emissions of ammonia (NH3), and a mixture of di-nitrogen 11 46|11 46|(N2), nitrogen oxide and dioxide (NOx) and nitrous oxide (N20). These change the actual amount of N 12 47|12 47|available when applied to land and hence the fertiliser value. Here, we use turkey litter as an 13 48|13 48|example of the consequences of using the litter as fuel, instead of applying it to land. Similar 14 49|14 49|arguments apply to the use of broiler litter as fuel, but we had better access to key aspects of turkey 16 15 50|16 15 50|litter management activity data and hence report this rather than broiler litter. 17 51|17 51|UK turkey annual production increased from the 1970s to a maximum of 40 M birds in 1995, steadily 18 52|18 52|fell to about 15 M in 2007, and then remained relatively constant (Defra 2014). The production of 20 19 53|20 19 53|turkey litter from 17.5 M slaughterings in 2013 was 0.9 Mt, which contained 21 kt N, 7.7 kt P (or 21 54|21 54|17.6 kt phosphorus pentoxide [P2O5]) and 13 kt K (or 15.7 kt potassium oxide [K2O]), using the 22 55|22 55|composition data from Defra (2010). There are poultry (of all species) across most of the country, 23 56|23 56|with higher concentrations in some parts, e.g. East Anglia, which also supports a large fraction of UK 24 57|24 57|turkey production (Figure 1). This has contributed to pressures in the nitrate vulnerable zones (NVZ) 25 58|25 58|that were introduced as implementation of the 1991 EU nitrates directive, which became more 27 26 59|27 26 59|stringent since being introduced. 28 60|28 60|Two important features affect the application of manure as a fertiliser: an upper limit on N applied 30 29 61|30 29 61|per ha and the exclusion of applications in late autumn and early winter (Defra, 2013). With high 31 62|31 62|concentrations of poultry in some areas, finding an alternative to land application consequently 32 63|32 63|became attractive to farmers. This was enhanced by the potential of using litter as a fuel, which 33 64|33 64|added to the UK's use of renewable biomass for generating electricity. Misselbrook, Pers. Comm.). Poultry litter 37 67|37 67|has been used in the Netherlands for electricity generation since 2008 (BMC Moerdijk (2008), Lynch 38 68|38 68|et al. (2013) and Quiroga et al. (2010) and investigated the properties of poultry litter as fuel by 39 69|39 69|direct combustion. Jia and Anthony (2011) examined co-combustion of poultry litter and coal. A typical UK turkey production system was used as a framework of the analysis (Leinonen et al., 2015)." 31 32 93|2. METHODS 33| 34 94 35|2.1. Scope of the LCA 36 95|The system boundary included all feed production (with associated upstream inputs) and husbandry 37 96 38|activities to produce turkeys up to the farm gate. The alternative approaches to manure 39 97|management were included within the system boundary. The functional unit (FU) was 1000 kg 40 98|turkey live weight at the farm, ready for slaughter. 41 42 99|2.2. Systems approach and the turkey production data 43 44 100|Some empirical activity data was supplied from the turkey industry and other partners, but systems 45 101|modelling was used to provide most the inputs for life cycle assessment This included structural 46 102|models of the industry, process models and simulation models that were unified in the systems 47 103|approach such that changes in one area caused consistent interactions elsewhere (Williams et al., 48 104 49|2006). This approach was applied to all subsystems modelled, including crop production, non-crop 50 105|nutrient production, feed processing, turkey breeding, turkey brooding and finishing plus manure 51 106|and general waste management, following the approaches in Leinonen et al. (2014), Williams et al., 52 107|(2006), Leinonen et al. (2012a) and Leinonen et al. (2012b). 53 54 108|A typical, mainstream UK turkey production system was thus represented, producing predominantly 55 109|stags (males) in controlled-ventilated houses (Leinonen et al., 2014). Farm energy consumption for 56 110|heating, lighting, ventilation and feeding was based on average data from typical farms. Liquefied 57 111 58|petroleum gas (LPG) was used for heating and electricity for everything else. Information on types 112 59|and amounts of bedding and other materials used was also obtained from the industry. The bird 60 113|performance and production data, including the length of the production cycle, stocking density, 61| 62| 63|Page 4 of 20 64|Page 4 of 20
Table 1

final weight, feed intake and mortality came also from actual farm data. The main production figures are shown in Table 1 and include average bird performance and feed intake and farm energy and material consumption. Additional data, e.g. life cycle inventories (LCI) of machinery, came from Williams et al., (2006). Representative baseline diets were used in the model and these were constructed using turkey industry data (using five-stage phase-feeding). UK-grown wheat was the main energy source in all diets and South American soya bean meal was the main protein source. In addition, the nutrient content of the diets was balanced with minerals and pure amino acids as additives, and a small amount of vegetable oil as an additional energy source. The environmental impacts arising from feed production were calculated based on the relative proportion of each ingredient in the overall diets and its modelled life cycle inventory (LCI) values, obtained with a separate crop model (Williams et al., 2006, 2010).
The average production data for the turkey system modelled in this study.

Age at slaughter, days|133 Weight at slaughter, kg|15.2 Feed conversion ratio, kg feed per kg live weight|3.1 Farm electricity use, kWh per slaughtered bird|3.5 Farm LPG use, litres per slaughtered bird|1.6 Bedding used, kg per slaughtered bird|4.4

N, P and K contents of the manure were calculated by the mass balance principle, i.e. the nutrients retained in the animal body were subtracted from the total amount of nutrients supplied by the feed. A separate sub-model was also used for manure and the nutrient cycle, in which the main nutrients applied to the soil in manure were accounted for as either crop products or losses to the environment. The benefits of N, P and K remaining in soil after land application of manure were credited to poultry by offsetting the need to apply fertilizers to winter wheat as described below.

A sub-model quantified the environmental impacts of the main feed ingredients from primary production to processing and delivery (Williams et al, 2010). Greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (GHGE) from direct land use change were accounted for using the principles of the carbon footprinting method PAS 2050:2011 (BSI, 2011) as applied in Leinonen et al. (2013).

Scope of the LCA 36 95|The system boundary included all feed production (with associated upstream inputs) and husbandry 37 96 38|activities to produce turkeys up to the farm gate. The alternative approaches to manure 39 97|management were included within the system boundary. The functional unit (FU) was 1000 kg 40 98|turkey live weight at the farm, ready for slaughter. 41 42 99|2.2. Systems approach and the turkey production data 43 44 100|Some empirical activity data was supplied from the turkey industry and other partners, but systems 45 101|modelling was used to provide most the inputs for life cycle assessment This included structural 46 102|models of the industry, process models and simulation models that were unified in the systems 47 103|approach such that changes in one area caused consistent interactions elsewhere (Williams et al., 48 104 49|2006). This approach was applied to all subsystems modelled, including crop production, non-crop 50 105|nutrient production, feed processing, turkey breeding, turkey brooding and finishing plus manure 51 106|and general waste management, following the approaches in Leinonen et al. (2014), Williams et al., 52 107|(2006), Leinonen et al. (2012a) and Leinonen et al. (2012b). 53 54 108|A typical, mainstream UK turkey production system was thus represented, producing predominantly 55 109|stags (males) in controlled-ventilated houses (Leinonen et al., 2014). Farm energy consumption for 56 110|heating, lighting, ventilation and feeding was based on average data from typical farms. Liquefied 57 111 58|petroleum gas (LPG) was used for heating and electricity for everything else. Information on types 112 59|and amounts of bedding and other materials used was also obtained from the industry. Table 1

final weight, feed intake and mortality came also from actual farm data. The main production figures are shown in Table 1 and include average bird performance and feed intake and farm energy and material consumption. Additional data, e.g. life cycle inventories (LCI) of machinery, came from Williams et al., (2006). Representative baseline diets were used in the model and these were constructed using turkey industry data (using five-stage phase-feeding). UK-grown wheat was the main energy source in all diets and South American soya bean meal was the main protein source. In addition, the nutrient content of the diets was balanced with minerals and pure amino acids as additives, and a small amount of vegetable oil as an additional energy source. The environmental impacts arising from feed production were calculated based on the relative proportion of each ingredient in the overall diets and its modelled life cycle inventory (LCI) values, obtained with a separate crop model (Williams et al., 2006, 2010).
The average production data for the turkey system modelled in this study.

N, P and K contents of the manure were calculated by the mass balance principle, i.e. the nutrients retained in the animal body were subtracted from the total amount of nutrients supplied by the feed. A separate sub-model was also used for manure and the nutrient cycle, in which the main nutrients applied to the soil in manure were accounted for as either crop products or losses to the environment. The benefits of N, P and K remaining in soil after land application of manure were credited to poultry by offsetting the need to apply fertilizers to winter wheat as described below.

A sub-model quantified the environmental impacts of the main feed ingredients from primary production to processing and delivery (Williams et al, 2010). Greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions (GHGE) from direct land use change were accounted for using the principles of the carbon footprinting method PAS 2050:2011 (BSI, 2011) as applied in Leinonen et al. (2013).

The main production figures are shown in Table 1 and include average bird performance and feed intake and farm energy and material consumption. Additional data, e.g. life cycle inventories (LCI) of machinery, came from Williams et al., (2006). Representative baseline diets were used in the model and these were constructed using turkey industry data (using five-stage phase-feeding). UK-grown wheat was the main energy source in all diets and South American soya bean meal was the main protein source. In addition, the nutrient content of the diets was balanced with minerals and pure amino acids as additives, and a small amount of vegetable oil as an additional energy source. The environmental impacts arising from feed production were calculated based on the relative proportion of each ingredient in the overall diets and its modelled life cycle inventory (LCI) values, obtained with a separate crop model (Williams et al., 2006, 2010).

16: <header>2.3. Modelling the use of poultry litter as fertilizer </header>

Quantifying the environmental burdens of manure management followed the principles of Sandars et al. (2003) as implemented by Williams et al. (2006). All the emissions from the time of excretion to land spreading are summed to derive the negative impacts against the livestock (or debits). The energy used for machinery manufacture and diesel used in manure management are also debited; the latter includes transport to arable farms and litter spreading. The N in manure has a benefit of increasing crop yield, and is treated in two parts in the model. Plant available N (PAN) is the sum of ammoniacal-N, uric acid-N and 10% of the remaining organic N (Org-N). PAN is assumed to be available to crops in the year of application (Chambers et al., 1999) and the residual Org-N gradually degrades over time to become PAN.

The long-term yield response of winter wheat to manure N is modelled as a combined effect of the PAN and Org-N, along with associated long-term losses from leaching and denitrification. The yield increment is equated to the same yield response from a mass of ammonium nitrate in non-organic systems. This is credited back to livestock as the burden of producing, delivering and applying the same mass of ammonium nitrate along with full credits for the P and K in manure, which displaces the need for equivalent amounts of manufactured P and K.

17: <header>2.3. Modelling the use of poultry litter as fertilizer </header>

Quantifying the environmental burdens of manure management followed the principles of Sandars et al. (2003) as implemented by Williams et al. (2006). All the emissions from the time of excretion to land spreading are summed to derive the negative impacts against the livestock (or debits). The energy used for machinery manufacture and diesel used in manure management are also debited; the latter includes transport to arable farms and litter spreading. The N in manure has a benefit of increasing crop yield, and is treated in two parts in the model. Plant available N (PAN) is the sum of ammoniacal-N, uric acid-N and 10% of the remaining organic N (Org-N). PAN is assumed to be available to crops in the year of application (Chambers et al., 1999) and the residual Org-N gradually degrades over time to become PAN.

The long-term yield response of winter wheat to manure N is modelled as a combined effect of the PAN and Org-N, along with associated long-term losses from leaching and denitrification. The yield increment is equated to the same yield response from a mass of ammonium nitrate in non-organic systems. This is credited back to livestock as the burden of producing, delivering and applying the same mass of ammonium nitrate along with full credits for the P and K in manure, which displaces the need for equivalent amounts of manufactured P and K.

20: <header>2.3. Modelling the use of poultry litter as fertilizer </header>

Quantifying the environmental burdens of manure management followed the principles of Sandars et al. (2003) as implemented by Williams et al. (2006). All the emissions from the time of excretion to land spreading are summed to derive the negative impacts against the livestock (or debits). The energy used for machinery manufacture and diesel used in manure management are also debited; the latter includes transport to arable farms and litter spreading. The N in manure has a benefit of increasing crop yield, and is treated in two parts in the model. Plant available N (PAN) is the sum of ammoniacal-N, uric acid-N and 10% of the remaining organic N (Org-N). PAN is assumed to be available to crops in the year of application (Chambers et al., 1999) and the residual Org-N gradually degrades over time to become PAN.

The long-term yield response of winter wheat to manure N is modelled as a combined effect of the PAN and Org-N, along with associated long-term losses from leaching and denitrification. The yield increment is equated to the same yield response from a mass of ammonium nitrate in non-organic systems. This is credited back to livestock as the burden of producing, delivering and applying the same mass of ammonium nitrate along with full credits for the P and K in manure, which displaces the need for equivalent amounts of manufactured P and K.

154|The litter is split into proportions that can be applied directly either without storage or (mainly) with 1 155|storage. The proportions of these two components were assumed to be those used in the UK 2 156|ammonia inventory (T.H. Misselbrook, Pers. Comm.). It can be expected that some storage of litter is 3 157 4|inevitable given nitrate vulnerable zone regulations (Defra, 2013) and the general wish to apply litter 158 5|when it is most suitable agronomically. 6 159|The effects of organic matter in litter and associated benefits for soil carbon are addressed in section 7 160 8|2.4.3. 9 161|In summary, the burdens from emissions and energy use for litter management are balanced against 10 162 11|the benefits of offsetting the need for manufactured fertilisers by using the plant nutrients in litter. 12 163 13|2.4. 23: <header>2.3. Modelling the use of poultry litter as fertilizer </header>

Quantifying the environmental burdens of manure management followed the principles of Sandars et al. (2003) as implemented by Williams et al. (2006). All the emissions from the time of excretion to land spreading are summed to derive the negative impacts against the livestock (or debits). The energy used for machinery manufacture and diesel used in manure management are also debited; the latter includes transport to arable farms and litter spreading. The N in manure has a benefit of increasing crop yield, and is treated in two parts in the model. Plant available N (PAN) is the sum of ammoniacal-N, uric acid-N and 10% of the remaining organic N (Org-N). PAN is assumed to be available to crops in the year of application (Chambers et al., 1999) and the residual Org-N gradually degrades over time to become PAN.

The long-term yield response of winter wheat to manure N is modelled as a combined effect of the PAN and Org-N, along with associated long-term losses from leaching and denitrification. The yield increment is equated to the same yield response from a mass of ammonium nitrate in non-organic systems. This is credited back to livestock as the burden of producing, delivering and applying the same mass of ammonium nitrate along with full credits for the P and K in manure, which displaces the need for equivalent amounts of manufactured P and K.

154|The litter is split into proportions that can be applied directly either without storage or (mainly) with 1 155|storage. The proportions of these two components were assumed to be those used in the UK 2 156|ammonia inventory (T.H. Misselbrook, Pers. Comm.). It can be expected that some storage of litter is 3 157 4|inevitable given nitrate vulnerable zone regulations (Defra, 2013) and the general wish to apply litter 158 5|when it is most suitable agronomically. 6 159|The effects of organic matter in litter and associated benefits for soil carbon are addressed in section 7 160 8|2.4.3. 9 161|In summary, the burdens from emissions and energy use for litter management are balanced against 10 162 11|the benefits of offsetting the need for manufactured fertilisers by using the plant nutrients in litter. 12 163 13|2.4. 26: <header>2.3. Modelling the use of poultry litter as fertilizer </header>

Quantifying the environmental burdens of manure management followed the principles of Sandars et al. (2003) as implemented by Williams et al. (2006). All the emissions from the time of excretion to land spreading are summed to derive the negative impacts against the livestock (or debits). The energy used for machinery manufacture and diesel used in manure management are also debited; the latter includes transport to arable farms and litter spreading. The N in manure has a benefit of increasing crop yield, and is treated in two parts in the model. Plant available N (PAN) is the sum of ammoniacal-N, uric acid-N and 10% of the remaining organic N (Org-N). PAN is assumed to be available to crops in the year of application (Chambers et al., 1999) and the residual Org-N gradually degrades over time to become PAN.

The long-term yield response of winter wheat to manure N is modelled as a combined effect of the PAN and Org-N, along with associated long-term losses from leaching and denitrification. The yield increment is equated to the same yield response from a mass of ammonium nitrate in non-organic systems. This is credited back to livestock as the burden of producing, delivering and applying the same mass of ammonium nitrate along with full credits for the P and K in manure, which displaces the need for equivalent amounts of manufactured P and K.

154|The litter is split into proportions that can be applied directly either without storage or (mainly) with 1 155|storage. The proportions of these two components were assumed to be those used in the UK 2 156|ammonia inventory (T.H. Misselbrook, Pers. Comm.). It can be expected that some storage of litter is 3 157 4|inevitable given nitrate vulnerable zone regulations (Defra, 2013) and the general wish to apply litter 158 5|when it is most suitable agronomically. 6 159|The effects of organic matter in litter and associated benefits for soil carbon are addressed in section 7 160 8|2.4.3. 9 161|In summary, the burdens from emissions and energy use for litter management are balanced against 10 162 11|the benefits of offsetting the need for manufactured fertilisers by using the plant nutrients in litter. 12 163 13|2.4. 29: <header>2.3. Modelling the use of poultry litter as fertilizer </header>

Quantifying the environmental burdens of manure management followed the principles of Sandars et al. (2003) as implemented by Williams et al. (2006). All the emissions from the time of excretion to land spreading are summed to derive the negative impacts against the livestock (or debits). The energy used for machinery manufacture and diesel used in manure management are also debited; the latter includes transport to arable farms and litter spreading. The N in manure has a benefit of increasing crop yield, and is treated in two parts in the model. Plant available N (PAN) is the sum of ammoniacal-N, uric acid-N and 10% of the remaining organic N (Org-N). PAN is assumed to be available to crops in the year of application (Chambers et al., 1999) and the residual Org-N gradually degrades over time to become PAN.

The long-term yield response of winter wheat to manure N is modelled as a combined effect of the PAN and Org-N, along with associated long-term losses from leaching and denitrification. The yield increment is equated to the same yield response from a mass of ammonium nitrate in non-organic systems. This is credited back to livestock as the burden of producing, delivering and applying the same mass of ammonium nitrate along with full credits for the P and K in manure, which displaces the need for equivalent amounts of manufactured P and K.

31: In summary, the burdens of transporting litter to power stations, trace combustion emissions, loss of soil C and transporting P and K in ash to farms are balanced against the reduced need for natural gas to generate electricity, lower ammonia emissions from manure storage and the reduced need for manufactured P and K fertiliser.
32: <header>2.5. Other data and environmental impacts categories </header>
The life cycle inventory data not previously specified, e.g. for diesel and natural gas, came from the European Life Cycle Database (ELCD, 2014).
Emissions to the environment were aggregated into environmentally functional groups as follows.
Global Warming Potential (GWP) was calculated using a timescale of 100 years, with 1 kg CH4 and N2O equivalent to 25 and 298 kg CO2 (COe) respectively (Forster et al., 2007).

Eutrophication Potential (EP) was calculated using the method of the Institute of Environmental Sciences (CML) at Leiden University (CML, 2014). The main sources in turkey production are nitrate (NO3) and phosphate (PO4 leaching to water and ammonia (NH3) emissions to air. EP was quantified as phosphate equivalents.

Acidification Potential (AP) was also calculated using the CML( 2014) data. The main sources are ammonia emissions, together with sulphur dioxide (SO2) from fossil fuel combustion. AP was quantified in terms of SO2 equivalents.
Dioxin emissions were quantified as 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin equivalents (CML, 2014).
Photochemical ozone creation potential (POCP) was quantified as ethylene equivalents (CML, 2014).
Particles smaller than 10 microns in diameter (PM10) were summed from all data sources used.

Dioxin emissions were quantified as 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin equivalents (CML, 2014).

0: In addition, energy use was quantified as cumulative energy demand (CED). This is the sum of all primary energy used and includes all the energy needed for extraction and supply of energy carriers, such as diesel or electricity (CML, 2014).

In the sensitivity analysis, all other variables were held constant while one variable under scrutiny was scaled up and down by 10% (Table 3). It was assumed that turkey production per se was unchanged so that only variables relating to litter management were tested.
In practice, the sensitivity analysis was linear in that 10% increases or decreases in inputs results in changes of equal magnitude (within 0.5%), thus only the effects of increases are reported.
Variables tested in the sensitivity analysis.

In the sensitivity analysis, all other variables were held constant while one variable under scrutiny was scaled up and down by 10% (Table 3). It was assumed that turkey production per se was unchanged so that only variables relating to litter management were tested.

0: <title>3.1. Effect of the use of litter as fuel on soil C content </title>

The time needed to reach a 90% change towards equilibrium (tgo) ranged from 174 to 373 years. Hence, New equilibria of soil C were established (or close to establishment) within 500 years after stopping the use of poultry litter as fertilizer for all soils investigated (Figure 2). This is, however, a very long time over which to assume a constant practice. A 20-year timescale is used in PAS2050 (BSI, 2011) and sustainability reporting within the Renewable Transport Fuel Obligation (DFT, 2011). The results show that the differences between the proportions of C lost from soil were relatively slow and small between 20 and 500 years (Figure 2, Table 4).

The mean of the changes over 20 year was thus in further analysis as this was consistent with the timescale used in PAS2050 and DFT (2011) and the scale is similar to the lifetime time of a power plant, during whic h this alternative use of litter is likely to occur.
Time since stopping manure application, years
Figure 2 Soil C content after stopping litter applications of 1 t FW/year, as simulated with RothC at 5 sites growing winter wheat in England, UK.
Soil C from litter lost and retained in soil derived from simulations with RothC. Results are the means of five sites.
Time, years|20|50|100|250|500 Proportion of C lost from litter|85%|87%|89%|93%|96% Coefficient of Variation|2%|2%|2%|2%|2%

The decline in soil C density is related to the proportion of litter C that is retained in soil. Over 20 years, the loss of soil C is equivalent to 28% of C in litter that would have been applied in the original equilibrium state, i.e. 0.053 t C [t litter]¹ This was converted to kg COe as 0.053 * 1000 * 44/12, i.e. 195 kg COe [t litter]¹. The net effect of this analysis was to add a further 338 kg CO2e emissions from soils per t live weight of turkeys (i.e. arising from 1.73 t litter per t live weight).

Effect of the litter management scenarios on environmental impact categories 3 4 287|The results show substantial environmental benefits from using litter as a fuel compared with direct 5 288|land application as fertiliser (Table 5). The biggest relative effects are from eutrophication and 6 289|acidification potentials (about 55% and 70% reductions respectively), which result from eliminating 7 290|ammonia emissions during storage and land spreading, summing to a 75% reduction overall. The 8 291 9|saving in cumulative energy demand (which is 99.5% non-renewable with litter as a fertiliser) is 10 292|about 14% with the fuel use option and this is reflected in a 14% decrease in abiotic resource use. 11 293|The proportion of renewable energy of the total energy use in turkey production increased to 2%. 12 294|The effect on reducing GHGE (or the carbon footprint), is, however small at only 3%. The largest 13 295|contribution to the effect on reducing CED is from offsetting gas for generating electricity. In 14 296 15|contrast, the saving in GHGE from electricity generation is the same magnitude as the loss of soil C 16 297|from not applying litter to land. Hence, the net effect was relatively small. 17 298|"There were also some negative effects of using litter as a fuel: NOx emissions increased by 64%, NMVOC by 12%, PM10 by 8% and dioxins by 4%. 1: <title>3.1. Effect of the use of litter as fuel on soil C content </title>

The time needed to reach a 90% change towards equilibrium (tgo) ranged from 174 to 373 years. Hence, New equilibria of soil C were established (or close to establishment) within 500 years after stopping the use of poultry litter as fertilizer for all soils investigated (Figure 2). This is, however, a very long time over which to assume a constant practice. A 20-year timescale is used in PAS2050 (BSI, 2011) and sustainability reporting within the Renewable Transport Fuel Obligation (DFT, 2011). The results show that the differences between the proportions of C lost from soil were relatively slow and small between 20 and 500 years (Figure 2, Table 4).

The mean of the changes over 20 year was thus in further analysis as this was consistent with the timescale used in PAS2050 and DFT (2011) and the scale is similar to the lifetime time of a power plant, during whic h this alternative use of litter is likely to occur.

0: The effects of changing litter management variables were examined for the effects on the alternative impacts per unit mass of litter and on the overall outcome of the LCA with the functional unit of 1000 kg LW (Section 2.6). Note that some values are negative, because of the balances in litter management, e.g. fertiliser value vs management activity for litter as fertiliser. Thus, increasing an input may decrease the output. The signs are shown for completeness, but the magnitudes are arguably more important.

For litter as fertiliser, changing the N concentration in litter had an even effect across most burdens, while increasing the litter transport distance had the largest effect on NOx and otherwise had similar effects changing to N concentrations except for categories related to ammonia and PM 10 (Table 7). The PK fertiliser value had most effect on CED, but also had large effects on dioxins, PM 10 and NOx.

With litter as fuel, the electricity exported had large effects on most burdens, except for dioxins (Table 8). The main influence on GWP was loss of soil C, which did not affect other categories and was of a similar magnitude to electricity export, but with the opposite sign. The PK fertiliser value of litter had the largest effects on ammonia related burdens and dioxins. The two transport terms had the least effects.

Some similar trends were seen when considering the responses of the whole LCA (Table 9), but these were generally of similar magnitude, given the large constant terms from turkey production per se. The N concentration in litter for fertiliser had the largest effect on ammonia and related impacts, including the largest single response with eutrophication potential. Electricity export had

the largest effect on CED and had the second highest effect on GWP after soil C. All three transport terms all had relatively small effects, with NOx being the largest, although electricity exported had the largest effect on NOx.

1| 2 357|Substantial environmental benefits from using turkey litter as a fuel for generating electricity were 3 358|demonstrated. We know of no directly comparable studies, despite the widespread use of the 4 359 5|practice. Huang et al. (2015) examined the economics and technical feasibility of using poultry litter 6 360|in Northern Ireland as a biofuel though pyrolysis and gasification, but this was based on simulations 7 361|modelling and did not quantify GHG emissions, although it suggested considerable promise in the 8 362|approach. The practice, however, and its consequences are frequently questioned both in the 9 363|popular press and trade literature (e.g. NFU, 2014). Reijnders and Huijbregts (2005) addressed GHG 10 364 11|emissions from burning animal wastes in Europe, including animal meal and litter. Much focus was 12 365|on the extra fossil fuel needed to produce the litter. They obtained contrasting results from either 13 366|energy-based or economic allocation. In contrast, we used the avoided burdens method, which 14 367|removes the arbitrariness of allocation and hence gives a more robust result (BSI, 2006). 15 16 368|There are concerns about trace gas emissions around power stations, which were examined by 17 369|Henihan et al. (2003). They found that ground level concentrations of NOx, SO2 and CO were all 18 370|within the limits and guidelines for air quality around a combustion plant. They did not, however, 19 371|quantify dioxin emissions, which were marginally increased in our study with litter combustion. We 20 372 21|have shown that most dioxin emissions, however, do not occur at the point source of the power 22 373|station. In contrast, most carbon monoxide and about half of NOx emissions are concentrated at the 23 374|power station. This agrees with the findings of Henihan et al. (2003). Nonetheless, existing power 24 375|stations in Britain are licenced, monitored and regulated by the respective environment agencies. It 25 376|is thus reasonable to deduce that no significant health hazard exists, but it is clear that great care 26 377 27|must be taken over the management and monitoring of such facilities. There is no room for 378 28|complacency. 29 379|Although the principal environmental interest of many stakeholders is the carbon footprint, the 30 380 31|benefits for reducing GHG emissions are small when poultry litter is used as fuel. The benefit of 32 381|reduced CO2 emissions from electricity production is roughly balanced by the loss of soil C. However, 33 382|the benefits for reducing CED and non-renewable energy use are substantially higher (and are thus 34 383|linked with abiotic resource use). There is still a clear dependency on fossil energy in the background 35 384|processes that lead to delivery of litter to the power station. It is thus somewhat questionable 36 385 37|whether the term renewable energy can fully be applied to this process. 38 386|Our results for GHG emissions are less favourable for electricity generation than those of Billen et al. 39 387|(2015). There are two main reasons for this. They did not account for loss of soil C by removing litter 40 388 41|from land application and their main comparison was with coal-based electricity generation, which is 42 389|more GHG intensive than gas generation. Further, their substitution of synthetic N fertiliser was 43 390|based on the total N concentration in litter, whereas ours was based on the long-term yield response 44 391|from the N fractions in litter. The latter applies a more rigorous substitution of N and would thus 45 392 46|give litter lower credits for the avoided burdens of synthetic N production and use. 47 393|The main benefits of using litter as fuel are associated with reduced ammonia emissions and the 48 394|consequent effects on AP and EP, which are the major environmental impacts arising from poultry 49 395|production (Leinonen et al., 2012a; 2014; Leinonen and Williams, 2015). Land application of manure 50 396 51|also contributes to nitrate leaching and aquatic eutrophication. The Convention on Long-Range 52 397|Transboundary Air Pollution (CLRTAP) led to the 1999 Gothenburg Protocol to abate acidification, 53 398|eutrophication and ground-level ozone, which was implemented by the EU National Emission 54 399|Ceilings directive (2001/81/EC (NECD) 2001), thus all EU countries have binding targets to reduce 55 400|ammonia (and other) emissions and using litter as a fuel contributes to this. This agrees with the 56 401 57|findings of Billen et al. (2015). 58 402|A disadvantage of litter combustion is that less C gets into arable soils, which already lost C since the 59 403 60|Second World War, e.g. by major changes from mixed to dominantly arable farming (Bellamy et al. 61| 62| |
1: <title>3.3. Sensitivity analysis results </title>

The effects of changing litter management variables were examined for the effects on the alternative impacts per unit mass of litter and on the overall outcome of the LCA with the functional unit of 1000 kg LW (Section 2.6). Note that some values are negative, because of the balances in litter management, e.g. fertiliser value vs management activity for litter as fertiliser. Thus, increasing an input may decrease the output. The signs are shown for completeness, but the magnitudes are arguably more important.

For litter as fertiliser, changing the N concentration in litter had an even effect across most burdens, while increasing the litter transport distance had the largest effect on NOx and otherwise had similar effects changing to N concentrations except for categories related to ammonia and PM 10 (Table 7). The PK fertiliser value had most effect on CED, but also had large effects on dioxins, PM 10 and NOx.

With litter as fuel, the electricity exported had large effects on most burdens, except for dioxins (Table 8). The main influence on GWP was loss of soil C, which did not affect other categories and was of a similar magnitude to electricity export, but with the opposite sign. The PK fertiliser value of litter had the largest effects on ammonia related burdens and dioxins. The two transport terms had the least effects.

The benefit of 32 381|reduced CO2 emissions from electricity production is roughly balanced by the loss of soil C. However, 33 382|the benefits for reducing CED and non-renewable energy use are substantially higher (and are thus 34 383|linked with abiotic resource use). There is still a clear dependency on fossil energy in the background 35 384|processes that lead to delivery of litter to the power station. 3: <title>3.3. Sensitivity analysis results </title>

The effects of changing litter management variables were examined for the effects on the alternative impacts per unit mass of litter and on the overall outcome of the LCA with the functional unit of 1000 kg LW (Section 2.6). Note that some values are negative, because of the balances in litter management, e.g. fertiliser value vs management activity for litter as fertiliser. Thus, increasing an input may decrease the output. The signs are shown for completeness, but the magnitudes are arguably more important.

For litter as fertiliser, changing the N concentration in litter had an even effect across most burdens, while increasing the litter transport distance had the largest effect on NOx and otherwise had similar effects changing to N concentrations except for categories related to ammonia and PM 10 (Table 7). The PK fertiliser value had most effect on CED, but also had large effects on dioxins, PM 10 and NOx.

With litter as fuel, the electricity exported had large effects on most burdens, except for dioxins (Table 8). The main influence on GWP was loss of soil C, which did not affect other categories and was of a similar magnitude to electricity export, but with the opposite sign. The PK fertiliser value of litter had the largest effects on ammonia related burdens and dioxins. The two transport terms had the least effects.

1| 2 357|Substantial environmental benefits from using turkey litter as a fuel for generating electricity were 3 358|demonstrated. We know of no directly comparable studies, despite the widespread use of the 4 359 5|practice. Huang et al. (2015) examined the economics and technical feasibility of using poultry litter 6 360|in Northern Ireland as a biofuel though pyrolysis and gasification, but this was based on simulations 7 361|modelling and did not quantify GHG emissions, although it suggested considerable promise in the 8 362|approach. The practice, however, and its consequences are frequently questioned both in the 9 363|popular press and trade literature (e.g. NFU, 2014). Reijnders and Huijbregts (2005) addressed GHG 10 364 11|emissions from burning animal wastes in Europe, including animal meal and litter. Much focus was 12 365|on the extra fossil fuel needed to produce the litter. They obtained contrasting results from either 13 366|energy-based or economic allocation. In contrast, we used the avoided burdens method, which 14 367|removes the arbitrariness of allocation and hence gives a more robust result (BSI, 2006). 15 16 368|There are concerns about trace gas emissions around power stations, which were examined by 17 369|Henihan et al. (2003). They found that ground level concentrations of NOx, SO2 and CO were all 18 370|within the limits and guidelines for air quality around a combustion plant. They did not, however, 19 371|quantify dioxin emissions, which were marginally increased in our study with litter combustion. We 20 372 21|have shown that most dioxin emissions, however, do not occur at the point source of the power 22 373|station. In contrast, most carbon monoxide and about half of NOx emissions are concentrated at the 23 374|power station. This agrees with the findings of Henihan et al. (2003). Nonetheless, existing power 24 375|stations in Britain are licenced, monitored and regulated by the respective environment agencies. It 25 376|is thus reasonable to deduce that no significant health hazard exists, but it is clear that great care 26 377 27|must be taken over the management and monitoring of such facilities. There is no room for 378 28|complacency. 29 379|Although the principal environmental interest of many stakeholders is the carbon footprint, the 30 380 31|benefits for reducing GHG emissions are small when poultry litter is used as fuel. The benefit of 32 381|reduced CO2 emissions from electricity production is roughly balanced by the loss of soil C. However, 33 382|the benefits for reducing CED and non-renewable energy use are substantially higher (and are thus 34 383|linked with abiotic resource use). There is still a clear dependency on fossil energy in the background 35 384|processes that lead to delivery of litter to the power station. It is thus somewhat questionable 36 385 37|whether the term renewable energy can fully be applied to this process. 38 386|Our results for GHG emissions are less favourable for electricity generation than those of Billen et al. 39 387|(2015). There are two main reasons for this. They did not account for loss of soil C by removing litter 40 388 41|from land application and their main comparison was with coal-based electricity generation, which is 42 389|more GHG intensive than gas generation. Further, their substitution of synthetic N fertiliser was 43 390|based on the total N concentration in litter, whereas ours was based on the long-term yield response 44 391|from the N fractions in litter. The latter applies a more rigorous substitution of N and would thus 45 392 46|give litter lower credits for the avoided burdens of synthetic N production and use. 47 393|The main benefits of using litter as fuel are associated with reduced ammonia emissions and the 48 394|consequent effects on AP and EP, which are the major environmental impacts arising from poultry 49 395|production (Leinonen et al., 2012a; 2014; Leinonen and Williams, 2015). Land application of manure 50 396 51|also contributes to nitrate leaching and aquatic eutrophication. The Convention on Long-Range 52 397|Transboundary Air Pollution (CLRTAP) led to the 1999 Gothenburg Protocol to abate acidification, 53 398|eutrophication and ground-level ozone, which was implemented by the EU National Emission 54 399|Ceilings directive (2001/81/EC (NECD) 2001), thus all EU countries have binding targets to reduce 55 400|ammonia (and other) emissions and using litter as a fuel contributes to this. This agrees with the 56 401 57|findings of Billen et al. (2015). 58 402|A disadvantage of litter combustion is that less C gets into arable soils, which already lost C since the 59 403 60|Second World War, e.g. by major changes from mixed to dominantly arable farming (Bellamy et al. 61| 62| |
7: <title>3.3. Sensitivity analysis results </title>

The effects of changing litter management variables were examined for the effects on the alternative impacts per unit mass of litter and on the overall outcome of the LCA with the functional unit of 1000 kg LW (Section 2.6). Note that some values are negative, because of the balances in litter management, e.g. fertiliser value vs management activity for litter as fertiliser. Thus, increasing an input may decrease the output. The signs are shown for completeness, but the magnitudes are arguably more important.

For litter as fertiliser, changing the N concentration in litter had an even effect across most burdens, while increasing the litter transport distance had the largest effect on NOx and otherwise had similar effects changing to N concentrations except for categories related to ammonia and PM 10 (Table 7). The PK fertiliser value had most effect on CED, but also had large effects on dioxins, PM 10 and NOx.

With litter as fuel, the electricity exported had large effects on most burdens, except for dioxins (Table 8). The main influence on GWP was loss of soil C, which did not affect other categories and was of a similar magnitude to electricity export, but with the opposite sign. The PK fertiliser value of litter had the largest effects on ammonia related burdens and dioxins. The two transport terms had the least effects.

1| 2 357|Substantial environmental benefits from using turkey litter as a fuel for generating electricity were 3 358|demonstrated. We know of no directly comparable studies, despite the widespread use of the 4 359 5|practice. Huang et al. (2015) examined the economics and technical feasibility of using poultry litter 6 360|in Northern Ireland as a biofuel though pyrolysis and gasification, but this was based on simulations 7 361|modelling and did not quantify GHG emissions, although it suggested considerable promise in the 8 362|approach. The practice, however, and its consequences are frequently questioned both in the 9 363|popular press and trade literature (e.g. NFU, 2014). Reijnders and Huijbregts (2005) addressed GHG 10 364 11|emissions from burning animal wastes in Europe, including animal meal and litter. Much focus was 12 365|on the extra fossil fuel needed to produce the litter. They obtained contrasting results from either 13 366|energy-based or economic allocation. In contrast, we used the avoided burdens method, which 14 367|removes the arbitrariness of allocation and hence gives a more robust result (BSI, 2006). 15 16 368|There are concerns about trace gas emissions around power stations, which were examined by 17 369|Henihan et al. (2003). They found that ground level concentrations of NOx, SO2 and CO were all 18 370|within the limits and guidelines for air quality around a combustion plant. They did not, however, 19 371|quantify dioxin emissions, which were marginally increased in our study with litter combustion. We 20 372 21|have shown that most dioxin emissions, however, do not occur at the point source of the power 22 373|station. In contrast, most carbon monoxide and about half of NOx emissions are concentrated at the 23 374|power station. This agrees with the findings of Henihan et al. (2003). Nonetheless, existing power 24 375|stations in Britain are licenced, monitored and regulated by the respective environment agencies. It 25 376|is thus reasonable to deduce that no significant health hazard exists, but it is clear that great care 26 377 27|must be taken over the management and monitoring of such facilities. There is no room for 378 28|complacency. 29 379|Although the principal environmental interest of many stakeholders is the carbon footprint, the 30 380 31|benefits for reducing GHG emissions are small when poultry litter is used as fuel. The benefit of 32 381|reduced CO2 emissions from electricity production is roughly balanced by the loss of soil C. However, 33 382|the benefits for reducing CED and non-renewable energy use are substantially higher (and are thus 34 383|linked with abiotic resource use). There is still a clear dependency on fossil energy in the background 35 384|processes that lead to delivery of litter to the power station. It is thus somewhat questionable 36 385 37|whether the term renewable energy can fully be applied to this process. 38 386|Our results for GHG emissions are less favourable for electricity generation than those of Billen et al. 39 387|(2015). There are two main reasons for this. They did not account for loss of soil C by removing litter 40 388 41|from land application and their main comparison was with coal-based electricity generation, which is 42 389|more GHG intensive than gas generation. Further, their substitution of synthetic N fertiliser was 43 390|based on the total N concentration in litter, whereas ours was based on the long-term yield response 44 391|from the N fractions in litter. The latter applies a more rigorous substitution of N and would thus 45 392 46|give litter lower credits for the avoided burdens of synthetic N production and use. 47 393|The main benefits of using litter as fuel are associated with reduced ammonia emissions and the 48 394|consequent effects on AP and EP, which are the major environmental impacts arising from poultry 49 395|production (Leinonen et al., 2012a; 2014; Leinonen and Williams, 2015). Land application of manure 50 396 51|also contributes to nitrate leaching and aquatic eutrophication. This agrees with the 56 401 57|findings of Billen et al. 10: <title>3.3. Sensitivity analysis results </title>

The effects of changing litter management variables were examined for the effects on the alternative impacts per unit mass of litter and on the overall outcome of the LCA with the functional unit of 1000 kg LW (Section 2.6). Note that some values are negative, because of the balances in litter management, e.g. fertiliser value vs management activity for litter as fertiliser. Thus, increasing an input may decrease the output. The signs are shown for completeness, but the magnitudes are arguably more important.

For litter as fertiliser, changing the N concentration in litter had an even effect across most burdens, while increasing the litter transport distance had the largest effect on NOx and otherwise had similar effects changing to N concentrations except for categories related to ammonia and PM 10 (Table 7). The PK fertiliser value had most effect on CED, but also had large effects on dioxins, PM 10 and NOx.

With litter as fuel, the electricity exported had large effects on most burdens, except for dioxins (Table 8). The main influence on GWP was loss of soil C, which did not affect other categories and was of a similar magnitude to electricity export, but with the opposite sign. The PK fertiliser value of litter had the largest effects on ammonia related burdens and dioxins. The two transport terms had the least effects.

11: 404|2005). Soil C helps maintain structure and supports active microbial communities. It acts as a store 1 405|for C while management practices support it, but change in practices can cause subsequent losses of 2 406|stored soil C. The balance of GHGE shows that despite the loss of C in soil storage with combustion, 3 407 4|this does not lead to a net increase in GHG emissions when the whole production system is taken 5 408|into account. We only considered a range of soils from England, where most turkey production is 6 409|found. The rate of decay of C depends on the clay content of soil and climate. The balance may thus 7 410|be different in other areas. The sensitivity analysis clearly showed the importance of the loss of soil 8 411|C. It should, however, be noted that the expected changes in the soil C content may differ depending 9 412 10|on the assumptions of the soil C model used in the analysis. The RothC soil C model, which was 413 11|applied in this study, has been extensively used across the world and compared with measured 12 414|changes in diverse ecosystems, e.g. Cerri et al. (2003). This reinforces our confidence in the other 13 415|main term addressing the net GHG emissions, namely the loss of soil carbon as a result of alternative 14 416|use of poultry litter. 15 16 417|One limitation of the use of poultry litter as a fertiliser is its high nutrient concentration. Providing a 17 418|large supply of C (to maintain the soil C storage) will be limited by the danger of an oversupply of N, 18 419 19|P and K. Indeed, the high N concentration in litter is one motive for using litter as a fuel. 20 420|The benefits for ammonia emissions are considerable when the litter is used as fuel. The current use 21 421|of turkey litter as a fuel saves about 0.9 kt ammonia emissions a year in the UK. This would be 22 422|increased by about a further 1.3 kt if litter use for fuel increase to 80% compared with the current 23 423 24|estimate of 35%. This would be about four times higher if applied also to broiler litter. 25 424|The scale of poultry farming in parts of eastern England has been an economic success for the 26 425 27|industry, but this has contributed to some environmental problems and, later, solutions. High 426 28|ammonia emissions from litter are one problem as well as eutrophication. The high concentrations 29 427|of poultry in parts of eastern England (plus other available biomass, like straw) also renders the 30 428|solution of fuel use an economic success, which also has environmental benefits. A more dispersed 31 429|poultry industry would lead to a less favourable structure for centralised fuel use, with increased 32 430|transport burdens. However, on-farm fuel use of litter has also become possible in Britain recently 33 431 34|through a change in the definition of litter in the waste management regulations. The main driver 35 432|behind on-farm use is to replace heating fuel with heat from litter. A broadly similar outcome would 36 433|occur if the small-scale fuel use of litter was analysed in the same way as with centralised power 37 434|stations. There would be different substitutions, e.g. fertiliser value for heating energy, and no extra 38 435 39|transport requirement, so relative magnitudes would differ. 40 436|Uncertainties of the estimated environmental impacts of UK turkey production were previously 41 437|quantified by Leinonen et al. (2015) but these are not reported quantitatively in this study. We 42 438 43|consider that there are too many areas in which the uncertainties of the model inputs are 439 44|unavailable to make a formal uncertainty analysis. However, we have high quality activity data, with 45 440|the litter collection and bird production data from the largest UK turkey producers, which accounts 46 441|for about 90% of housed UK turkey production. Litter use by power stations is recorded publicly and 47 442|used in other areas that require robust data, e.g. the UK GHG inventory. The turkey production 48 443|systems model is based on extensive data from the industry, including bird growth rates, diets and 49 444 50|resource use. The main input variable of the model, i.e. the amount of electricity generated per unit 445 51|of combusted litter was checked against other data sources. Quiroga et al. (2010) found that the 52 446|lower heating value of laying poultry manure was 12.1 MJ [kg DM] hence being 5.8 MJ [kg FW]¹ at 53 447|48% DM. Yassin et al. (2009) analysed fluidised-bed biomass combustion and found an energy 54 448|efficiency of 22%, giving at electricity output of 0.35 kWh electricity [kg FW]¹ 1 which is close to the 55 449 56|value given by ERPL of 0.31 kWh electricity [kg FW] This gives considerable confidence in the 450 57|analysis. 58 451|As discussed above, turkey litter was used as the basis of this analysis, because of the high quality 59 452 60|activity data available to us. CONCLUSIONS 4 5 456|As far as we are aware, this is the first study that systematically compares the environmental 6 457|impacts of using turkey litter either generate electricity instead or using turkey litter as fertilizer and 7 458 8|including turkey production. The results show that there are substantial environmental benefits 459 9|from using turkey litter as a fuel to rather than using it directly as a fertiliser. The main benefits are 10 460|from reduced ammonia emissions and saving primary fossil energy by generating electricity from 11 461|biomass. There is still a substantial input of fossil energy into the electricity produced resulting from 12 462|use in turkey production and litter collection. The very small decrease in greenhouse gas emissions 13 463|is the result of a fine balance between the loss of soil C and the displacement of electricity from a 14 464 15|marginal combined cycle gas turbine generator. There were, however, small increases in NMVOC, 16 465|NOx, dioxin, and PM- 10 emissions and such emissions must be managed and regulated in the most 17 466|stringent way to avoid any hazard to health. The increases in dioxins and PM 10 were not related to 18 467|combustion emissions, whereas those of NOx clearly were. 19 20 468|6. 12: 404|2005). Soil C helps maintain structure and supports active microbial communities. It acts as a store 1 405|for C while management practices support it, but change in practices can cause subsequent losses of 2 406|stored soil C. The balance of GHGE shows that despite the loss of C in soil storage with combustion, 3 407 4|this does not lead to a net increase in GHG emissions when the whole production system is taken 5 408|into account. We only considered a range of soils from England, where most turkey production is 6 409|found. The rate of decay of C depends on the clay content of soil and climate. The balance may thus 7 410|be different in other areas. The sensitivity analysis clearly showed the importance of the loss of soil 8 411|C. It should, however, be noted that the expected changes in the soil C content may differ depending 9 412 10|on the assumptions of the soil C model used in the analysis. The RothC soil C model, which was 413 11|applied in this study, has been extensively used across the world and compared with measured 12 414|changes in diverse ecosystems, e.g. Cerri et al. (2003). This reinforces our confidence in the other 13 415|main term addressing the net GHG emissions, namely the loss of soil carbon as a result of alternative 14 416|use of poultry litter. 15 16 417|One limitation of the use of poultry litter as a fertiliser is its high nutrient concentration. Providing a 17 418|large supply of C (to maintain the soil C storage) will be limited by the danger of an oversupply of N, 18 419 19|P and K. Indeed, the high N concentration in litter is one motive for using litter as a fuel. 20 420|The benefits for ammonia emissions are considerable when the litter is used as fuel. The current use 21 421|of turkey litter as a fuel saves about 0.9 kt ammonia emissions a year in the UK. This would be 22 422|increased by about a further 1.3 kt if litter use for fuel increase to 80% compared with the current 23 423 24|estimate of 35%. This would be about four times higher if applied also to broiler litter. 25 424|The scale of poultry farming in parts of eastern England has been an economic success for the 26 425 27|industry, but this has contributed to some environmental problems and, later, solutions. High 426 28|ammonia emissions from litter are one problem as well as eutrophication. The high concentrations 29 427|of poultry in parts of eastern England (plus other available biomass, like straw) also renders the 30 428|solution of fuel use an economic success, which also has environmental benefits. A more dispersed 31 429|poultry industry would lead to a less favourable structure for centralised fuel use, with increased 32 430|transport burdens. However, on-farm fuel use of litter has also become possible in Britain recently 33 431 34|through a change in the definition of litter in the waste management regulations. The main driver 35 432|behind on-farm use is to replace heating fuel with heat from litter. A broadly similar outcome would 36 433|occur if the small-scale fuel use of litter was analysed in the same way as with centralised power 37 434|stations. There would be different substitutions, e.g. fertiliser value for heating energy, and no extra 38 435 39|transport requirement, so relative magnitudes would differ. 40 436|Uncertainties of the estimated environmental impacts of UK turkey production were previously 41 437|quantified by Leinonen et al. (2015) but these are not reported quantitatively in this study. We 42 438 43|consider that there are too many areas in which the uncertainties of the model inputs are 439 44|unavailable to make a formal uncertainty analysis. However, we have high quality activity data, with 45 440|the litter collection and bird production data from the largest UK turkey producers, which accounts 46 441|for about 90% of housed UK turkey production. Litter use by power stations is recorded publicly and 47 442|used in other areas that require robust data, e.g. the UK GHG inventory. The turkey production 48 443|systems model is based on extensive data from the industry, including bird growth rates, diets and 49 444 50|resource use. The main input variable of the model, i.e. the amount of electricity generated per unit 445 51|of combusted litter was checked against other data sources. Quiroga et al. (2010) found that the 52 446|lower heating value of laying poultry manure was 12.1 MJ [kg DM] hence being 5.8 MJ [kg FW]¹ at 53 447|48% DM. Yassin et al. (2009) analysed fluidised-bed biomass combustion and found an energy 54 448|efficiency of 22%, giving at electricity output of 0.35 kWh electricity [kg FW]¹ 1 which is close to the 55 449 56|value given by ERPL of 0.31 kWh electricity [kg FW] This gives considerable confidence in the 450 57|analysis. 58 451|As discussed above, turkey litter was used as the basis of this analysis, because of the high quality 59 452 60|activity data available to us. CONCLUSIONS 4 5 456|As far as we are aware, this is the first study that systematically compares the environmental 6 457|impacts of using turkey litter either generate electricity instead or using turkey litter as fertilizer and 7 458 8|including turkey production. The results show that there are substantial environmental benefits 459 9|from using turkey litter as a fuel to rather than using it directly as a fertiliser. The main benefits are 10 460|from reduced ammonia emissions and saving primary fossil energy by generating electricity from 11 461|biomass. There is still a substantial input of fossil energy into the electricity produced resulting from 12 462|use in turkey production and litter collection. The very small decrease in greenhouse gas emissions 13 463|is the result of a fine balance between the loss of soil C and the displacement of electricity from a 14 464 15|marginal combined cycle gas turbine generator. There were, however, small increases in NMVOC, 16 465|NOx, dioxin, and PM- 10 emissions and such emissions must be managed and regulated in the most 17 466|stringent way to avoid any hazard to health. The increases in dioxins and PM 10 were not related to 18 467|combustion emissions, whereas those of NOx clearly were. 19 20 468|6. 13: 404|2005). Soil C helps maintain structure and supports active microbial communities. It acts as a store 1 405|for C while management practices support it, but change in practices can cause subsequent losses of 2 406|stored soil C. The balance of GHGE shows that despite the loss of C in soil storage with combustion, 3 407 4|this does not lead to a net increase in GHG emissions when the whole production system is taken 5 408|into account. We only considered a range of soils from England, where most turkey production is 6 409|found. The rate of decay of C depends on the clay content of soil and climate. The balance may thus 7 410|be different in other areas. The sensitivity analysis clearly showed the importance of the loss of soil 8 411|C. It should, however, be noted that the expected changes in the soil C content may differ depending 9 412 10|on the assumptions of the soil C model used in the analysis. The RothC soil C model, which was 413 11|applied in this study, has been extensively used across the world and compared with measured 12 414|changes in diverse ecosystems, e.g. Cerri et al. (2003). This reinforces our confidence in the other 13 415|main term addressing the net GHG emissions, namely the loss of soil carbon as a result of alternative 14 416|use of poultry litter. 15 16 417|One limitation of the use of poultry litter as a fertiliser is its high nutrient concentration. Providing a 17 418|large supply of C (to maintain the soil C storage) will be limited by the danger of an oversupply of N, 18 419 19|P and K. Indeed, the high N concentration in litter is one motive for using litter as a fuel. 20 420|The benefits for ammonia emissions are considerable when the litter is used as fuel. The current use 21 421|of turkey litter as a fuel saves about 0.9 kt ammonia emissions a year in the UK. This would be 22 422|increased by about a further 1.3 kt if litter use for fuel increase to 80% compared with the current 23 423 24|estimate of 35%. This would be about four times higher if applied also to broiler litter. 25 424|The scale of poultry farming in parts of eastern England has been an economic success for the 26 425 27|industry, but this has contributed to some environmental problems and, later, solutions. High 426 28|ammonia emissions from litter are one problem as well as eutrophication. The high concentrations 29 427|of poultry in parts of eastern England (plus other available biomass, like straw) also renders the 30 428|solution of fuel use an economic success, which also has environmental benefits. A more dispersed 31 429|poultry industry would lead to a less favourable structure for centralised fuel use, with increased 32 430|transport burdens. However, on-farm fuel use of litter has also become possible in Britain recently 33 431 34|through a change in the definition of litter in the waste management regulations. The main driver 35 432|behind on-farm use is to replace heating fuel with heat from litter. A broadly similar outcome would 36 433|occur if the small-scale fuel use of litter was analysed in the same way as with centralised power 37 434|stations. There would be different substitutions, e.g. fertiliser value for heating energy, and no extra 38 435 39|transport requirement, so relative magnitudes would differ. 40 436|Uncertainties of the estimated environmental impacts of UK turkey production were previously 41 437|quantified by Leinonen et al. (2015) but these are not reported quantitatively in this study. We 42 438 43|consider that there are too many areas in which the uncertainties of the model inputs are 439 44|unavailable to make a formal uncertainty analysis. However, we have high quality activity data, with 45 440|the litter collection and bird production data from the largest UK turkey producers, which accounts 46 441|for about 90% of housed UK turkey production. Litter use by power stations is recorded publicly and 47 442|used in other areas that require robust data, e.g. the UK GHG inventory. The turkey production 48 443|systems model is based on extensive data from the industry, including bird growth rates, diets and 49 444 50|resource use. The main input variable of the model, i.e. the amount of electricity generated per unit 445 51|of combusted litter was checked against other data sources. Quiroga et al. (2010) found that the 52 446|lower heating value of laying poultry manure was 12.1 MJ [kg DM] hence being 5.8 MJ [kg FW]¹ at 53 447|48% DM. Yassin et al. (2009) analysed fluidised-bed biomass combustion and found an energy 54 448|efficiency of 22%, giving at electricity output of 0.35 kWh electricity [kg FW]¹ 1 which is close to the 55 449 56|value given by ERPL of 0.31 kWh electricity [kg FW] This gives considerable confidence in the 450 57|analysis. 58 451|As discussed above, turkey litter was used as the basis of this analysis, because of the high quality 59 452 60|activity data available to us.
