26: 404|2005). Soil C helps maintain structure and supports active microbial communities. It acts as a store 1 405|for C while management practices support it, but change in practices can cause subsequent losses of 2 406|stored soil C. The balance of GHGE shows that despite the loss of C in soil storage with combustion, 3 407 4|this does not lead to a net increase in GHG emissions when the whole production system is taken 5 408|into account. We only considered a range of soils from England, where most turkey production is 6 409|found. The rate of decay of C depends on the clay content of soil and climate. The balance may thus 7 410|be different in other areas. The sensitivity analysis clearly showed the importance of the loss of soil 8 411|C. It should, however, be noted that the expected changes in the soil C content may differ depending 9 412 10|on the assumptions of the soil C model used in the analysis. The RothC soil C model, which was 413 11|applied in this study, has been extensively used across the world and compared with measured 12 414|changes in diverse ecosystems, e.g. Cerri et al. (2003). This reinforces our confidence in the other 13 415|main term addressing the net GHG emissions, namely the loss of soil carbon as a result of alternative 14 416|use of poultry litter. 15 16 417|One limitation of the use of poultry litter as a fertiliser is its high nutrient concentration. Providing a 17 418|large supply of C (to maintain the soil C storage) will be limited by the danger of an oversupply of N, 18 419 19|P and K. Indeed, the high N concentration in litter is one motive for using litter as a fuel. 20 420|The benefits for ammonia emissions are considerable when the litter is used as fuel. The current use 21 421|of turkey litter as a fuel saves about 0.9 kt ammonia emissions a year in the UK. This would be 22 422|increased by about a further 1.3 kt if litter use for fuel increase to 80% compared with the current 23 423 24|estimate of 35%. This would be about four times higher if applied also to broiler litter. 25 424|The scale of poultry farming in parts of eastern England has been an economic success for the 26 425 27|industry, but this has contributed to some environmental problems and, later, solutions. High 426 28|ammonia emissions from litter are one problem as well as eutrophication. The high concentrations 29 427|of poultry in parts of eastern England (plus other available biomass, like straw) also renders the 30 428|solution of fuel use an economic success, which also has environmental benefits. A more dispersed 31 429|poultry industry would lead to a less favourable structure for centralised fuel use, with increased 32 430|transport burdens. However, on-farm fuel use of litter has also become possible in Britain recently 33 431 34|through a change in the definition of litter in the waste management regulations. The main driver 35 432|behind on-farm use is to replace heating fuel with heat from litter. A broadly similar outcome would 36 433|occur if the small-scale fuel use of litter was analysed in the same way as with centralised power 37 434|stations. There would be different substitutions, e.g. fertiliser value for heating energy, and no extra 38 435 39|transport requirement, so relative magnitudes would differ. 40 436|Uncertainties of the estimated environmental impacts of UK turkey production were previously 41 437|quantified by Leinonen et al. (2015) but these are not reported quantitatively in this study. We 42 438 43|consider that there are too many areas in which the uncertainties of the model inputs are 439 44|unavailable to make a formal uncertainty analysis. However, we have high quality activity data, with 45 440|the litter collection and bird production data from the largest UK turkey producers, which accounts 46 441|for about 90% of housed UK turkey production. Litter use by power stations is recorded publicly and 47 442|used in other areas that require robust data, e.g. the UK GHG inventory. The turkey production 48 443|systems model is based on extensive data from the industry, including bird growth rates, diets and 49 444 50|resource use. The main input variable of the model, i.e. the amount of electricity generated per unit 445 51|of combusted litter was checked against other data sources. Quiroga et al. (2010) found that the 52 446|lower heating value of laying poultry manure was 12.1 MJ [kg DM] hence being 5.8 MJ [kg FW]¹ at 53 447|48% DM. Yassin et al. (2009) analysed fluidised-bed biomass combustion and found an energy 54 448|efficiency of 22%, giving at electricity output of 0.35 kWh electricity [kg FW]¹ 1 which is close to the 55 449 56|value given by ERPL of 0.31 kWh electricity [kg FW] This gives considerable confidence in the 450 57|analysis. 58 451|As discussed above, turkey litter was used as the basis of this analysis, because of the high quality 59 452 60|activity data available to us. CONCLUSIONS 4 5 456|As far as we are aware, this is the first study that systematically compares the environmental 6 457|impacts of using turkey litter either generate electricity instead or using turkey litter as fertilizer and 7 458 8|including turkey production. The results show that there are substantial environmental benefits 459 9|from using turkey litter as a fuel to rather than using it directly as a fertiliser. The main benefits are 10 460|from reduced ammonia emissions and saving primary fossil energy by generating electricity from 11 461|biomass. There is still a substantial input of fossil energy into the electricity produced resulting from 12 462|use in turkey production and litter collection. The very small decrease in greenhouse gas emissions 13 463|is the result of a fine balance between the loss of soil C and the displacement of electricity from a 14 464 15|marginal combined cycle gas turbine generator. There were, however, small increases in NMVOC, 16 465|NOx, dioxin, and PM- 10 emissions and such emissions must be managed and regulated in the most 17 466|stringent way to avoid any hazard to health. The increases in dioxins and PM 10 were not related to 18 467|combustion emissions, whereas those of NOx clearly were. 19 20 468|6. 27: 404|2005). Soil C helps maintain structure and supports active microbial communities. It acts as a store 1 405|for C while management practices support it, but change in practices can cause subsequent losses of 2 406|stored soil C. The balance of GHGE shows that despite the loss of C in soil storage with combustion, 3 407 4|this does not lead to a net increase in GHG emissions when the whole production system is taken 5 408|into account. We only considered a range of soils from England, where most turkey production is 6 409|found. The rate of decay of C depends on the clay content of soil and climate. The balance may thus 7 410|be different in other areas. The sensitivity analysis clearly showed the importance of the loss of soil 8 411|C. It should, however, be noted that the expected changes in the soil C content may differ depending 9 412 10|on the assumptions of the soil C model used in the analysis. The RothC soil C model, which was 413 11|applied in this study, has been extensively used across the world and compared with measured 12 414|changes in diverse ecosystems, e.g. Cerri et al. (2003). This reinforces our confidence in the other 13 415|main term addressing the net GHG emissions, namely the loss of soil carbon as a result of alternative 14 416|use of poultry litter. 15 16 417|One limitation of the use of poultry litter as a fertiliser is its high nutrient concentration. Providing a 17 418|large supply of C (to maintain the soil C storage) will be limited by the danger of an oversupply of N, 18 419 19|P and K. Indeed, the high N concentration in litter is one motive for using litter as a fuel. 20 420|The benefits for ammonia emissions are considerable when the litter is used as fuel. The current use 21 421|of turkey litter as a fuel saves about 0.9 kt ammonia emissions a year in the UK. This would be 22 422|increased by about a further 1.3 kt if litter use for fuel increase to 80% compared with the current 23 423 24|estimate of 35%. This would be about four times higher if applied also to broiler litter. 25 424|The scale of poultry farming in parts of eastern England has been an economic success for the 26 425 27|industry, but this has contributed to some environmental problems and, later, solutions. High 426 28|ammonia emissions from litter are one problem as well as eutrophication. The high concentrations 29 427|of poultry in parts of eastern England (plus other available biomass, like straw) also renders the 30 428|solution of fuel use an economic success, which also has environmental benefits. A more dispersed 31 429|poultry industry would lead to a less favourable structure for centralised fuel use, with increased 32 430|transport burdens. However, on-farm fuel use of litter has also become possible in Britain recently 33 431 34|through a change in the definition of litter in the waste management regulations. The main driver 35 432|behind on-farm use is to replace heating fuel with heat from litter. A broadly similar outcome would 36 433|occur if the small-scale fuel use of litter was analysed in the same way as with centralised power 37 434|stations. There would be different substitutions, e.g. fertiliser value for heating energy, and no extra 38 435 39|transport requirement, so relative magnitudes would differ. 40 436|Uncertainties of the estimated environmental impacts of UK turkey production were previously 41 437|quantified by Leinonen et al. (2015) but these are not reported quantitatively in this study. We 42 438 43|consider that there are too many areas in which the uncertainties of the model inputs are 439 44|unavailable to make a formal uncertainty analysis. However, we have high quality activity data, with 45 440|the litter collection and bird production data from the largest UK turkey producers, which accounts 46 441|for about 90% of housed UK turkey production. Litter use by power stations is recorded publicly and 47 442|used in other areas that require robust data, e.g. the UK GHG inventory. The turkey production 48 443|systems model is based on extensive data from the industry, including bird growth rates, diets and 49 444 50|resource use. The main input variable of the model, i.e. the amount of electricity generated per unit 445 51|of combusted litter was checked against other data sources. Quiroga et al. (2010) found that the 52 446|lower heating value of laying poultry manure was 12.1 MJ [kg DM] hence being 5.8 MJ [kg FW]¹ at 53 447|48% DM. Yassin et al. (2009) analysed fluidised-bed biomass combustion and found an energy 54 448|efficiency of 22%, giving at electricity output of 0.35 kWh electricity [kg FW]¹ 1 which is close to the 55 449 56|value given by ERPL of 0.31 kWh electricity [kg FW] This gives considerable confidence in the 450 57|analysis. 58 451|As discussed above, turkey litter was used as the basis of this analysis, because of the high quality 59 452 60|activity data available to us. CONCLUSIONS 4 5 456|As far as we are aware, this is the first study that systematically compares the environmental 6 457|impacts of using turkey litter either generate electricity instead or using turkey litter as fertilizer and 7 458 8|including turkey production. The results show that there are substantial environmental benefits 459 9|from using turkey litter as a fuel to rather than using it directly as a fertiliser. The main benefits are 10 460|from reduced ammonia emissions and saving primary fossil energy by generating electricity from 11 461|biomass. There is still a substantial input of fossil energy into the electricity produced resulting from 12 462|use in turkey production and litter collection. The very small decrease in greenhouse gas emissions 13 463|is the result of a fine balance between the loss of soil C and the displacement of electricity from a 14 464 15|marginal combined cycle gas turbine generator. There were, however, small increases in NMVOC, 16 465|NOx, dioxin, and PM- 10 emissions and such emissions must be managed and regulated in the most 17 466|stringent way to avoid any hazard to health. The increases in dioxins and PM 10 were not related to 18 467|combustion emissions, whereas those of NOx clearly were. 19 20 468|6. 28: 404|2005). Soil C helps maintain structure and supports active microbial communities. It acts as a store 1 405|for C while management practices support it, but change in practices can cause subsequent losses of 2 406|stored soil C. The balance of GHGE shows that despite the loss of C in soil storage with combustion, 3 407 4|this does not lead to a net increase in GHG emissions when the whole production system is taken 5 408|into account. We only considered a range of soils from England, where most turkey production is 6 409|found. The rate of decay of C depends on the clay content of soil and climate. The balance may thus 7 410|be different in other areas. The sensitivity analysis clearly showed the importance of the loss of soil 8 411|C. It should, however, be noted that the expected changes in the soil C content may differ depending 9 412 10|on the assumptions of the soil C model used in the analysis. The RothC soil C model, which was 413 11|applied in this study, has been extensively used across the world and compared with measured 12 414|changes in diverse ecosystems, e.g. Cerri et al. (2003). This reinforces our confidence in the other 13 415|main term addressing the net GHG emissions, namely the loss of soil carbon as a result of alternative 14 416|use of poultry litter. 15 16 417|One limitation of the use of poultry litter as a fertiliser is its high nutrient concentration. Providing a 17 418|large supply of C (to maintain the soil C storage) will be limited by the danger of an oversupply of N, 18 419 19|P and K. Indeed, the high N concentration in litter is one motive for using litter as a fuel. 20 420|The benefits for ammonia emissions are considerable when the litter is used as fuel. The current use 21 421|of turkey litter as a fuel saves about 0.9 kt ammonia emissions a year in the UK. This would be 22 422|increased by about a further 1.3 kt if litter use for fuel increase to 80% compared with the current 23 423 24|estimate of 35%. This would be about four times higher if applied also to broiler litter. 25 424|The scale of poultry farming in parts of eastern England has been an economic success for the 26 425 27|industry, but this has contributed to some environmental problems and, later, solutions. High 426 28|ammonia emissions from litter are one problem as well as eutrophication. The high concentrations 29 427|of poultry in parts of eastern England (plus other available biomass, like straw) also renders the 30 428|solution of fuel use an economic success, which also has environmental benefits. A more dispersed 31 429|poultry industry would lead to a less favourable structure for centralised fuel use, with increased 32 430|transport burdens. However, on-farm fuel use of litter has also become possible in Britain recently 33 431 34|through a change in the definition of litter in the waste management regulations. The main driver 35 432|behind on-farm use is to replace heating fuel with heat from litter. A broadly similar outcome would 36 433|occur if the small-scale fuel use of litter was analysed in the same way as with centralised power 37 434|stations. There would be different substitutions, e.g. fertiliser value for heating energy, and no extra 38 435 39|transport requirement, so relative magnitudes would differ. 40 436|Uncertainties of the estimated environmental impacts of UK turkey production were previously 41 437|quantified by Leinonen et al. (2015) but these are not reported quantitatively in this study. We 42 438 43|consider that there are too many areas in which the uncertainties of the model inputs are 439 44|unavailable to make a formal uncertainty analysis. However, we have high quality activity data, with 45 440|the litter collection and bird production data from the largest UK turkey producers, which accounts 46 441|for about 90% of housed UK turkey production. Litter use by power stations is recorded publicly and 47 442|used in other areas that require robust data, e.g. the UK GHG inventory. The turkey production 48 443|systems model is based on extensive data from the industry, including bird growth rates, diets and 49 444 50|resource use. The main input variable of the model, i.e. the amount of electricity generated per unit 445 51|of combusted litter was checked against other data sources. Quiroga et al. (2010) found that the 52 446|lower heating value of laying poultry manure was 12.1 MJ [kg DM] hence being 5.8 MJ [kg FW]¹ at 53 447|48% DM. Yassin et al. (2009) analysed fluidised-bed biomass combustion and found an energy 54 448|efficiency of 22%, giving at electricity output of 0.35 kWh electricity [kg FW]¹ 1 which is close to the 55 449 56|value given by ERPL of 0.31 kWh electricity [kg FW] This gives considerable confidence in the 450 57|analysis. 58 451|As discussed above, turkey litter was used as the basis of this analysis, because of the high quality 59 452 60|activity data available to us. CONCLUSIONS 4 5 456|As far as we are aware, this is the first study that systematically compares the environmental 6 457|impacts of using turkey litter either generate electricity instead or using turkey litter as fertilizer and 7 458 8|including turkey production. The results show that there are substantial environmental benefits 459 9|from using turkey litter as a fuel to rather than using it directly as a fertiliser. The main benefits are 10 460|from reduced ammonia emissions and saving primary fossil energy by generating electricity from 11 461|biomass. There is still a substantial input of fossil energy into the electricity produced resulting from 12 462|use in turkey production and litter collection. The very small decrease in greenhouse gas emissions 13 463|is the result of a fine balance between the loss of soil C and the displacement of electricity from a 14 464 15|marginal combined cycle gas turbine generator. There were, however, small increases in NMVOC, 16 465|NOx, dioxin, and PM- 10 emissions and such emissions must be managed and regulated in the most 17 466|stringent way to avoid any hazard to health. The increases in dioxins and PM 10 were not related to 18 467|combustion emissions, whereas those of NOx clearly were. 19 20 468|6. 29: 404|2005). Soil C helps maintain structure and supports active microbial communities. It acts as a store 1 405|for C while management practices support it, but change in practices can cause subsequent losses of 2 406|stored soil C. The balance of GHGE shows that despite the loss of C in soil storage with combustion, 3 407 4|this does not lead to a net increase in GHG emissions when the whole production system is taken 5 408|into account. We only considered a range of soils from England, where most turkey production is 6 409|found. The rate of decay of C depends on the clay content of soil and climate. The balance may thus 7 410|be different in other areas. The sensitivity analysis clearly showed the importance of the loss of soil 8 411|C. It should, however, be noted that the expected changes in the soil C content may differ depending 9 412 10|on the assumptions of the soil C model used in the analysis. The RothC soil C model, which was 413 11|applied in this study, has been extensively used across the world and compared with measured 12 414|changes in diverse ecosystems, e.g. Cerri et al. (2003). This reinforces our confidence in the other 13 415|main term addressing the net GHG emissions, namely the loss of soil carbon as a result of alternative 14 416|use of poultry litter. 15 16 417|One limitation of the use of poultry litter as a fertiliser is its high nutrient concentration. Providing a 17 418|large supply of C (to maintain the soil C storage) will be limited by the danger of an oversupply of N, 18 419 19|P and K. Indeed, the high N concentration in litter is one motive for using litter as a fuel. 20 420|The benefits for ammonia emissions are considerable when the litter is used as fuel. The current use 21 421|of turkey litter as a fuel saves about 0.9 kt ammonia emissions a year in the UK. This would be 22 422|increased by about a further 1.3 kt if litter use for fuel increase to 80% compared with the current 23 423 24|estimate of 35%. This would be about four times higher if applied also to broiler litter. 25 424|The scale of poultry farming in parts of eastern England has been an economic success for the 26 425 27|industry, but this has contributed to some environmental problems and, later, solutions. High 426 28|ammonia emissions from litter are one problem as well as eutrophication. The high concentrations 29 427|of poultry in parts of eastern England (plus other available biomass, like straw) also renders the 30 428|solution of fuel use an economic success, which also has environmental benefits. A more dispersed 31 429|poultry industry would lead to a less favourable structure for centralised fuel use, with increased 32 430|transport burdens. However, on-farm fuel use of litter has also become possible in Britain recently 33 431 34|through a change in the definition of litter in the waste management regulations. The main driver 35 432|behind on-farm use is to replace heating fuel with heat from litter. A broadly similar outcome would 36 433|occur if the small-scale fuel use of litter was analysed in the same way as with centralised power 37 434|stations. There would be different substitutions, e.g. fertiliser value for heating energy, and no extra 38 435 39|transport requirement, so relative magnitudes would differ. 40 436|Uncertainties of the estimated environmental impacts of UK turkey production were previously 41 437|quantified by Leinonen et al. (2015) but these are not reported quantitatively in this study. We 42 438 43|consider that there are too many areas in which the uncertainties of the model inputs are 439 44|unavailable to make a formal uncertainty analysis. However, we have high quality activity data, with 45 440|the litter collection and bird production data from the largest UK turkey producers, which accounts 46 441|for about 90% of housed UK turkey production. Litter use by power stations is recorded publicly and 47 442|used in other areas that require robust data, e.g. the UK GHG inventory. The turkey production 48 443|systems model is based on extensive data from the industry, including bird growth rates, diets and 49 444 50|resource use. The main input variable of the model, i.e. the amount of electricity generated per unit 445 51|of combusted litter was checked against other data sources. Quiroga et al. (2010) found that the 52 446|lower heating value of laying poultry manure was 12.1 MJ [kg DM] hence being 5.8 MJ [kg FW]¹ at 53 447|48% DM. Yassin et al. (2009) analysed fluidised-bed biomass combustion and found an energy 54 448|efficiency of 22%, giving at electricity output of 0.35 kWh electricity [kg FW]¹ 1 which is close to the 55 449 56|value given by ERPL of 0.31 kWh electricity [kg FW] This gives considerable confidence in the 450 57|analysis. 58 451|As discussed above, turkey litter was used as the basis of this analysis, because of the high quality 59 452 60|activity data available to us. CONCLUSIONS 4 5 456|As far as we are aware, this is the first study that systematically compares the environmental 6 457|impacts of using turkey litter either generate electricity instead or using turkey litter as fertilizer and 7 458 8|including turkey production. The results show that there are substantial environmental benefits 459 9|from using turkey litter as a fuel to rather than using it directly as a fertiliser. The main benefits are 10 460|from reduced ammonia emissions and saving primary fossil energy by generating electricity from 11 461|biomass. There is still a substantial input of fossil energy into the electricity produced resulting from 12 462|use in turkey production and litter collection. The very small decrease in greenhouse gas emissions 13 463|is the result of a fine balance between the loss of soil C and the displacement of electricity from a 14 464 15|marginal combined cycle gas turbine generator. There were, however, small increases in NMVOC, 16 465|NOx, dioxin, and PM- 10 emissions and such emissions must be managed and regulated in the most 17 466|stringent way to avoid any hazard to health. The increases in dioxins and PM 10 were not related to 18 467|combustion emissions, whereas those of NOx clearly were. 19 20 468|6. 30: 404|2005). Soil C helps maintain structure and supports active microbial communities. It acts as a store 1 405|for C while management practices support it, but change in practices can cause subsequent losses of 2 406|stored soil C. The balance of GHGE shows that despite the loss of C in soil storage with combustion, 3 407 4|this does not lead to a net increase in GHG emissions when the whole production system is taken 5 408|into account. We only considered a range of soils from England, where most turkey production is 6 409|found. The rate of decay of C depends on the clay content of soil and climate. The balance may thus 7 410|be different in other areas. The sensitivity analysis clearly showed the importance of the loss of soil 8 411|C. It should, however, be noted that the expected changes in the soil C content may differ depending 9 412 10|on the assumptions of the soil C model used in the analysis. The RothC soil C model, which was 413 11|applied in this study, has been extensively used across the world and compared with measured 12 414|changes in diverse ecosystems, e.g. Cerri et al. (2003). This reinforces our confidence in the other 13 415|main term addressing the net GHG emissions, namely the loss of soil carbon as a result of alternative 14 416|use of poultry litter. 15 16 417|One limitation of the use of poultry litter as a fertiliser is its high nutrient concentration. Providing a 17 418|large supply of C (to maintain the soil C storage) will be limited by the danger of an oversupply of N, 18 419 19|P and K. Indeed, the high N concentration in litter is one motive for using litter as a fuel. 20 420|The benefits for ammonia emissions are considerable when the litter is used as fuel. The current use 21 421|of turkey litter as a fuel saves about 0.9 kt ammonia emissions a year in the UK. This would be 22 422|increased by about a further 1.3 kt if litter use for fuel increase to 80% compared with the current 23 423 24|estimate of 35%. This would be about four times higher if applied also to broiler litter. 25 424|The scale of poultry farming in parts of eastern England has been an economic success for the 26 425 27|industry, but this has contributed to some environmental problems and, later, solutions. High 426 28|ammonia emissions from litter are one problem as well as eutrophication. The high concentrations 29 427|of poultry in parts of eastern England (plus other available biomass, like straw) also renders the 30 428|solution of fuel use an economic success, which also has environmental benefits. A more dispersed 31 429|poultry industry would lead to a less favourable structure for centralised fuel use, with increased 32 430|transport burdens. However, on-farm fuel use of litter has also become possible in Britain recently 33 431 34|through a change in the definition of litter in the waste management regulations. The main driver 35 432|behind on-farm use is to replace heating fuel with heat from litter. A broadly similar outcome would 36 433|occur if the small-scale fuel use of litter was analysed in the same way as with centralised power 37 434|stations. There would be different substitutions, e.g. fertiliser value for heating energy, and no extra 38 435 39|transport requirement, so relative magnitudes would differ. 40 436|Uncertainties of the estimated environmental impacts of UK turkey production were previously 41 437|quantified by Leinonen et al. (2015) but these are not reported quantitatively in this study. We 42 438 43|consider that there are too many areas in which the uncertainties of the model inputs are 439 44|unavailable to make a formal uncertainty analysis. However, we have high quality activity data, with 45 440|the litter collection and bird production data from the largest UK turkey producers, which accounts 46 441|for about 90% of housed UK turkey production. Litter use by power stations is recorded publicly and 47 442|used in other areas that require robust data, e.g. the UK GHG inventory. The turkey production 48 443|systems model is based on extensive data from the industry, including bird growth rates, diets and 49 444 50|resource use. The main input variable of the model, i.e. the amount of electricity generated per unit 445 51|of combusted litter was checked against other data sources. Quiroga et al. (2010) found that the 52 446|lower heating value of laying poultry manure was 12.1 MJ [kg DM] hence being 5.8 MJ [kg FW]¹ at 53 447|48% DM. Yassin et al. (2009) analysed fluidised-bed biomass combustion and found an energy 54 448|efficiency of 22%, giving at electricity output of 0.35 kWh electricity [kg FW]¹ 1 which is close to the 55 449 56|value given by ERPL of 0.31 kWh electricity [kg FW] This gives considerable confidence in the 450 57|analysis. 58 451|As discussed above, turkey litter was used as the basis of this analysis, because of the high quality 59 452 60|activity data available to us. CONCLUSIONS 4 5 456|As far as we are aware, this is the first study that systematically compares the environmental 6 457|impacts of using turkey litter either generate electricity instead or using turkey litter as fertilizer and 7 458 8|including turkey production. The results show that there are substantial environmental benefits 459 9|from using turkey litter as a fuel to rather than using it directly as a fertiliser. The main benefits are 10 460|from reduced ammonia emissions and saving primary fossil energy by generating electricity from 11 461|biomass. There is still a substantial input of fossil energy into the electricity produced resulting from 12 462|use in turkey production and litter collection. The very small decrease in greenhouse gas emissions 13 463|is the result of a fine balance between the loss of soil C and the displacement of electricity from a 14 464 15|marginal combined cycle gas turbine generator. There were, however, small increases in NMVOC, 16 465|NOx, dioxin, and PM- 10 emissions and such emissions must be managed and regulated in the most 17 466|stringent way to avoid any hazard to health. The increases in dioxins and PM 10 were not related to 18 467|combustion emissions, whereas those of NOx clearly were. 19 20 468|6. 31: 404|2005). Soil C helps maintain structure and supports active microbial communities. It acts as a store 1 405|for C while management practices support it, but change in practices can cause subsequent losses of 2 406|stored soil C. The balance of GHGE shows that despite the loss of C in soil storage with combustion, 3 407 4|this does not lead to a net increase in GHG emissions when the whole production system is taken 5 408|into account. We only considered a range of soils from England, where most turkey production is 6 409|found. The rate of decay of C depends on the clay content of soil and climate. The balance may thus 7 410|be different in other areas. The sensitivity analysis clearly showed the importance of the loss of soil 8 411|C. It should, however, be noted that the expected changes in the soil C content may differ depending 9 412 10|on the assumptions of the soil C model used in the analysis. The RothC soil C model, which was 413 11|applied in this study, has been extensively used across the world and compared with measured 12 414|changes in diverse ecosystems, e.g. Cerri et al. (2003). This reinforces our confidence in the other 13 415|main term addressing the net GHG emissions, namely the loss of soil carbon as a result of alternative 14 416|use of poultry litter. 15 16 417|One limitation of the use of poultry litter as a fertiliser is its high nutrient concentration. Providing a 17 418|large supply of C (to maintain the soil C storage) will be limited by the danger of an oversupply of N, 18 419 19|P and K. Indeed, the high N concentration in litter is one motive for using litter as a fuel. 20 420|The benefits for ammonia emissions are considerable when the litter is used as fuel. The current use 21 421|of turkey litter as a fuel saves about 0.9 kt ammonia emissions a year in the UK. This would be 22 422|increased by about a further 1.3 kt if litter use for fuel increase to 80% compared with the current 23 423 24|estimate of 35%. This would be about four times higher if applied also to broiler litter. 25 424|The scale of poultry farming in parts of eastern England has been an economic success for the 26 425 27|industry, but this has contributed to some environmental problems and, later, solutions. High 426 28|ammonia emissions from litter are one problem as well as eutrophication. The high concentrations 29 427|of poultry in parts of eastern England (plus other available biomass, like straw) also renders the 30 428|solution of fuel use an economic success, which also has environmental benefits. A more dispersed 31 429|poultry industry would lead to a less favourable structure for centralised fuel use, with increased 32 430|transport burdens. However, on-farm fuel use of litter has also become possible in Britain recently 33 431 34|through a change in the definition of litter in the waste management regulations. The main driver 35 432|behind on-farm use is to replace heating fuel with heat from litter. A broadly similar outcome would 36 433|occur if the small-scale fuel use of litter was analysed in the same way as with centralised power 37 434|stations. There would be different substitutions, e.g. fertiliser value for heating energy, and no extra 38 435 39|transport requirement, so relative magnitudes would differ. 40 436|Uncertainties of the estimated environmental impacts of UK turkey production were previously 41 437|quantified by Leinonen et al. (2015) but these are not reported quantitatively in this study. We 42 438 43|consider that there are too many areas in which the uncertainties of the model inputs are 439 44|unavailable to make a formal uncertainty analysis. However, we have high quality activity data, with 45 440|the litter collection and bird production data from the largest UK turkey producers, which accounts 46 441|for about 90% of housed UK turkey production. Litter use by power stations is recorded publicly and 47 442|used in other areas that require robust data, e.g. the UK GHG inventory. The turkey production 48 443|systems model is based on extensive data from the industry, including bird growth rates, diets and 49 444 50|resource use. The main input variable of the model, i.e. the amount of electricity generated per unit 445 51|of combusted litter was checked against other data sources. Quiroga et al. (2010) found that the 52 446|lower heating value of laying poultry manure was 12.1 MJ [kg DM] hence being 5.8 MJ [kg FW]¹ at 53 447|48% DM. Yassin et al. (2009) analysed fluidised-bed biomass combustion and found an energy 54 448|efficiency of 22%, giving at electricity output of 0.35 kWh electricity [kg FW]¹ 1 which is close to the 55 449 56|value given by ERPL of 0.31 kWh electricity [kg FW] This gives considerable confidence in the 450 57|analysis. 58 451|As discussed above, turkey litter was used as the basis of this analysis, because of the high quality 59 452 60|activity data available to us. CONCLUSIONS 4 5 456|As far as we are aware, this is the first study that systematically compares the environmental 6 457|impacts of using turkey litter either generate electricity instead or using turkey litter as fertilizer and 7 458 8|including turkey production. The results show that there are substantial environmental benefits 459 9|from using turkey litter as a fuel to rather than using it directly as a fertiliser. The main benefits are 10 460|from reduced ammonia emissions and saving primary fossil energy by generating electricity from 11 461|biomass. There is still a substantial input of fossil energy into the electricity produced resulting from 12 462|use in turkey production and litter collection. The very small decrease in greenhouse gas emissions 13 463|is the result of a fine balance between the loss of soil C and the displacement of electricity from a 14 464 15|marginal combined cycle gas turbine generator. There were, however, small increases in NMVOC, 16 465|NOx, dioxin, and PM- 10 emissions and such emissions must be managed and regulated in the most 17 466|stringent way to avoid any hazard to health. The increases in dioxins and PM 10 were not related to 18 467|combustion emissions, whereas those of NOx clearly were. 19 20 468|6. 32: 404|2005). Soil C helps maintain structure and supports active microbial communities. It acts as a store 1 405|for C while management practices support it, but change in practices can cause subsequent losses of 2 406|stored soil C. The balance of GHGE shows that despite the loss of C in soil storage with combustion, 3 407 4|this does not lead to a net increase in GHG emissions when the whole production system is taken 5 408|into account. We only considered a range of soils from England, where most turkey production is 6 409|found. The rate of decay of C depends on the clay content of soil and climate. The balance may thus 7 410|be different in other areas. The sensitivity analysis clearly showed the importance of the loss of soil 8 411|C. It should, however, be noted that the expected changes in the soil C content may differ depending 9 412 10|on the assumptions of the soil C model used in the analysis. The RothC soil C model, which was 413 11|applied in this study, has been extensively used across the world and compared with measured 12 414|changes in diverse ecosystems, e.g. Cerri et al. (2003). This reinforces our confidence in the other 13 415|main term addressing the net GHG emissions, namely the loss of soil carbon as a result of alternative 14 416|use of poultry litter. 15 16 417|One limitation of the use of poultry litter as a fertiliser is its high nutrient concentration. Providing a 17 418|large supply of C (to maintain the soil C storage) will be limited by the danger of an oversupply of N, 18 419 19|P and K. Indeed, the high N concentration in litter is one motive for using litter as a fuel. 20 420|The benefits for ammonia emissions are considerable when the litter is used as fuel. The current use 21 421|of turkey litter as a fuel saves about 0.9 kt ammonia emissions a year in the UK. This would be 22 422|increased by about a further 1.3 kt if litter use for fuel increase to 80% compared with the current 23 423 24|estimate of 35%. This would be about four times higher if applied also to broiler litter. 25 424|The scale of poultry farming in parts of eastern England has been an economic success for the 26 425 27|industry, but this has contributed to some environmental problems and, later, solutions. High 426 28|ammonia emissions from litter are one problem as well as eutrophication. The high concentrations 29 427|of poultry in parts of eastern England (plus other available biomass, like straw) also renders the 30 428|solution of fuel use an economic success, which also has environmental benefits. A more dispersed 31 429|poultry industry would lead to a less favourable structure for centralised fuel use, with increased 32 430|transport burdens. However, on-farm fuel use of litter has also become possible in Britain recently 33 431 34|through a change in the definition of litter in the waste management regulations. The main driver 35 432|behind on-farm use is to replace heating fuel with heat from litter. A broadly similar outcome would 36 433|occur if the small-scale fuel use of litter was analysed in the same way as with centralised power 37 434|stations. There would be different substitutions, e.g. fertiliser value for heating energy, and no extra 38 435 39|transport requirement, so relative magnitudes would differ. 40 436|Uncertainties of the estimated environmental impacts of UK turkey production were previously 41 437|quantified by Leinonen et al. (2015) but these are not reported quantitatively in this study. We 42 438 43|consider that there are too many areas in which the uncertainties of the model inputs are 439 44|unavailable to make a formal uncertainty analysis. However, we have high quality activity data, with 45 440|the litter collection and bird production data from the largest UK turkey producers, which accounts 46 441|for about 90% of housed UK turkey production. Litter use by power stations is recorded publicly and 47 442|used in other areas that require robust data, e.g. the UK GHG inventory. The turkey production 48 443|systems model is based on extensive data from the industry, including bird growth rates, diets and 49 444 50|resource use. The main input variable of the model, i.e. the amount of electricity generated per unit 445 51|of combusted litter was checked against other data sources. Quiroga et al. (2010) found that the 52 446|lower heating value of laying poultry manure was 12.1 MJ [kg DM] hence being 5.8 MJ [kg FW]¹ at 53 447|48% DM. Yassin et al. (2009) analysed fluidised-bed biomass combustion and found an energy 54 448|efficiency of 22%, giving at electricity output of 0.35 kWh electricity [kg FW]¹ 1 which is close to the 55 449 56|value given by ERPL of 0.31 kWh electricity [kg FW] This gives considerable confidence in the 450 57|analysis. 58 451|As discussed above, turkey litter was used as the basis of this analysis, because of the high quality 59 452 60|activity data available to us. CONCLUSIONS 4 5 456|As far as we are aware, this is the first study that systematically compares the environmental 6 457|impacts of using turkey litter either generate electricity instead or using turkey litter as fertilizer and 7 458 8|including turkey production. The results show that there are substantial environmental benefits 459 9|from using turkey litter as a fuel to rather than using it directly as a fertiliser. The main benefits are 10 460|from reduced ammonia emissions and saving primary fossil energy by generating electricity from 11 461|biomass. There is still a substantial input of fossil energy into the electricity produced resulting from 12 462|use in turkey production and litter collection. The very small decrease in greenhouse gas emissions 13 463|is the result of a fine balance between the loss of soil C and the displacement of electricity from a 14 464 15|marginal combined cycle gas turbine generator. There were, however, small increases in NMVOC, 16 465|NOx, dioxin, and PM- 10 emissions and such emissions must be managed and regulated in the most 17 466|stringent way to avoid any hazard to health. The increases in dioxins and PM 10 were not related to 18 467|combustion emissions, whereas those of NOx clearly were. 19 20 468|6. 33: 404|2005). Soil C helps maintain structure and supports active microbial communities. It acts as a store 1 405|for C while management practices support it, but change in practices can cause subsequent losses of 2 406|stored soil C. The balance of GHGE shows that despite the loss of C in soil storage with combustion, 3 407 4|this does not lead to a net increase in GHG emissions when the whole production system is taken 5 408|into account. We only considered a range of soils from England, where most turkey production is 6 409|found. The rate of decay of C depends on the clay content of soil and climate. The balance may thus 7 410|be different in other areas. The sensitivity analysis clearly showed the importance of the loss of soil 8 411|C. It should, however, be noted that the expected changes in the soil C content may differ depending 9 412 10|on the assumptions of the soil C model used in the analysis. The RothC soil C model, which was 413 11|applied in this study, has been extensively used across the world and compared with measured 12 414|changes in diverse ecosystems, e.g. Cerri et al. (2003). This reinforces our confidence in the other 13 415|main term addressing the net GHG emissions, namely the loss of soil carbon as a result of alternative 14 416|use of poultry litter. 15 16 417|One limitation of the use of poultry litter as a fertiliser is its high nutrient concentration. Providing a 17 418|large supply of C (to maintain the soil C storage) will be limited by the danger of an oversupply of N, 18 419 19|P and K. Indeed, the high N concentration in litter is one motive for using litter as a fuel. 20 420|The benefits for ammonia emissions are considerable when the litter is used as fuel. The current use 21 421|of turkey litter as a fuel saves about 0.9 kt ammonia emissions a year in the UK. This would be 22 422|increased by about a further 1.3 kt if litter use for fuel increase to 80% compared with the current 23 423 24|estimate of 35%. This would be about four times higher if applied also to broiler litter. 25 424|The scale of poultry farming in parts of eastern England has been an economic success for the 26 425 27|industry, but this has contributed to some environmental problems and, later, solutions. High 426 28|ammonia emissions from litter are one problem as well as eutrophication. The high concentrations 29 427|of poultry in parts of eastern England (plus other available biomass, like straw) also renders the 30 428|solution of fuel use an economic success, which also has environmental benefits. A more dispersed 31 429|poultry industry would lead to a less favourable structure for centralised fuel use, with increased 32 430|transport burdens. However, on-farm fuel use of litter has also become possible in Britain recently 33 431 34|through a change in the definition of litter in the waste management regulations. The main driver 35 432|behind on-farm use is to replace heating fuel with heat from litter. A broadly similar outcome would 36 433|occur if the small-scale fuel use of litter was analysed in the same way as with centralised power 37 434|stations. There would be different substitutions, e.g. fertiliser value for heating energy, and no extra 38 435 39|transport requirement, so relative magnitudes would differ. 40 436|Uncertainties of the estimated environmental impacts of UK turkey production were previously 41 437|quantified by Leinonen et al. (2015) but these are not reported quantitatively in this study. We 42 438 43|consider that there are too many areas in which the uncertainties of the model inputs are 439 44|unavailable to make a formal uncertainty analysis. However, we have high quality activity data, with 45 440|the litter collection and bird production data from the largest UK turkey producers, which accounts 46 441|for about 90% of housed UK turkey production. Litter use by power stations is recorded publicly and 47 442|used in other areas that require robust data, e.g. the UK GHG inventory. The turkey production 48 443|systems model is based on extensive data from the industry, including bird growth rates, diets and 49 444 50|resource use. The main input variable of the model, i.e. the amount of electricity generated per unit 445 51|of combusted litter was checked against other data sources. Quiroga et al. (2010) found that the 52 446|lower heating value of laying poultry manure was 12.1 MJ [kg DM] hence being 5.8 MJ [kg FW]¹ at 53 447|48% DM. Yassin et al. (2009) analysed fluidised-bed biomass combustion and found an energy 54 448|efficiency of 22%, giving at electricity output of 0.35 kWh electricity [kg FW]¹ 1 which is close to the 55 449 56|value given by ERPL of 0.31 kWh electricity [kg FW] This gives considerable confidence in the 450 57|analysis. 58 451|As discussed above, turkey litter was used as the basis of this analysis, because of the high quality 59 452 60|activity data available to us. CONCLUSIONS 4 5 456|As far as we are aware, this is the first study that systematically compares the environmental 6 457|impacts of using turkey litter either generate electricity instead or using turkey litter as fertilizer and 7 458 8|including turkey production. The results show that there are substantial environmental benefits 459 9|from using turkey litter as a fuel to rather than using it directly as a fertiliser. The main benefits are 10 460|from reduced ammonia emissions and saving primary fossil energy by generating electricity from 11 461|biomass. There is still a substantial input of fossil energy into the electricity produced resulting from 12 462|use in turkey production and litter collection. The very small decrease in greenhouse gas emissions 13 463|is the result of a fine balance between the loss of soil C and the displacement of electricity from a 14 464 15|marginal combined cycle gas turbine generator. There were, however, small increases in NMVOC, 16 465|NOx, dioxin, and PM- 10 emissions and such emissions must be managed and regulated in the most 17 466|stringent way to avoid any hazard to health. The increases in dioxins and PM 10 were not related to 18 467|combustion emissions, whereas those of NOx clearly were. 19 20 468|6. 34: 404|2005). Soil C helps maintain structure and supports active microbial communities. It acts as a store 1 405|for C while management practices support it, but change in practices can cause subsequent losses of 2 406|stored soil C. The balance of GHGE shows that despite the loss of C in soil storage with combustion, 3 407 4|this does not lead to a net increase in GHG emissions when the whole production system is taken 5 408|into account. We only considered a range of soils from England, where most turkey production is 6 409|found. The rate of decay of C depends on the clay content of soil and climate. The balance may thus 7 410|be different in other areas. The sensitivity analysis clearly showed the importance of the loss of soil 8 411|C. It should, however, be noted that the expected changes in the soil C content may differ depending 9 412 10|on the assumptions of the soil C model used in the analysis. The RothC soil C model, which was 413 11|applied in this study, has been extensively used across the world and compared with measured 12 414|changes in diverse ecosystems, e.g. Cerri et al. (2003). This reinforces our confidence in the other 13 415|main term addressing the net GHG emissions, namely the loss of soil carbon as a result of alternative 14 416|use of poultry litter. 15 16 417|One limitation of the use of poultry litter as a fertiliser is its high nutrient concentration. Providing a 17 418|large supply of C (to maintain the soil C storage) will be limited by the danger of an oversupply of N, 18 419 19|P and K. Indeed, the high N concentration in litter is one motive for using litter as a fuel. 20 420|The benefits for ammonia emissions are considerable when the litter is used as fuel. The current use 21 421|of turkey litter as a fuel saves about 0.9 kt ammonia emissions a year in the UK. This would be 22 422|increased by about a further 1.3 kt if litter use for fuel increase to 80% compared with the current 23 423 24|estimate of 35%. This would be about four times higher if applied also to broiler litter. 25 424|The scale of poultry farming in parts of eastern England has been an economic success for the 26 425 27|industry, but this has contributed to some environmental problems and, later, solutions. High 426 28|ammonia emissions from litter are one problem as well as eutrophication. The high concentrations 29 427|of poultry in parts of eastern England (plus other available biomass, like straw) also renders the 30 428|solution of fuel use an economic success, which also has environmental benefits. A more dispersed 31 429|poultry industry would lead to a less favourable structure for centralised fuel use, with increased 32 430|transport burdens. However, on-farm fuel use of litter has also become possible in Britain recently 33 431 34|through a change in the definition of litter in the waste management regulations. The main driver 35 432|behind on-farm use is to replace heating fuel with heat from litter. A broadly similar outcome would 36 433|occur if the small-scale fuel use of litter was analysed in the same way as with centralised power 37 434|stations. There would be different substitutions, e.g. fertiliser value for heating energy, and no extra 38 435 39|transport requirement, so relative magnitudes would differ. 40 436|Uncertainties of the estimated environmental impacts of UK turkey production were previously 41 437|quantified by Leinonen et al. (2015) but these are not reported quantitatively in this study. We 42 438 43|consider that there are too many areas in which the uncertainties of the model inputs are 439 44|unavailable to make a formal uncertainty analysis. However, we have high quality activity data, with 45 440|the litter collection and bird production data from the largest UK turkey producers, which accounts 46 441|for about 90% of housed UK turkey production. Litter use by power stations is recorded publicly and 47 442|used in other areas that require robust data, e.g. the UK GHG inventory. The turkey production 48 443|systems model is based on extensive data from the industry, including bird growth rates, diets and 49 444 50|resource use. The main input variable of the model, i.e. the amount of electricity generated per unit 445 51|of combusted litter was checked against other data sources. Quiroga et al. (2010) found that the 52 446|lower heating value of laying poultry manure was 12.1 MJ [kg DM] hence being 5.8 MJ [kg FW]¹ at 53 447|48% DM. Yassin et al. (2009) analysed fluidised-bed biomass combustion and found an energy 54 448|efficiency of 22%, giving at electricity output of 0.35 kWh electricity [kg FW]¹ 1 which is close to the 55 449 56|value given by ERPL of 0.31 kWh electricity [kg FW] This gives considerable confidence in the 450 57|analysis. 58 451|As discussed above, turkey litter was used as the basis of this analysis, because of the high quality 59 452 60|activity data available to us. CONCLUSIONS 4 5 456|As far as we are aware, this is the first study that systematically compares the environmental 6 457|impacts of using turkey litter either generate electricity instead or using turkey litter as fertilizer and 7 458 8|including turkey production. The results show that there are substantial environmental benefits 459 9|from using turkey litter as a fuel to rather than using it directly as a fertiliser. The main benefits are 10 460|from reduced ammonia emissions and saving primary fossil energy by generating electricity from 11 461|biomass. There is still a substantial input of fossil energy into the electricity produced resulting from 12 462|use in turkey production and litter collection. The very small decrease in greenhouse gas emissions 13 463|is the result of a fine balance between the loss of soil C and the displacement of electricity from a 14 464 15|marginal combined cycle gas turbine generator. There were, however, small increases in NMVOC, 16 465|NOx, dioxin, and PM- 10 emissions and such emissions must be managed and regulated in the most 17 466|stringent way to avoid any hazard to health. The increases in dioxins and PM 10 were not related to 18 467|combustion emissions, whereas those of NOx clearly were. 19 20 468|6. 35: 404|2005). Soil C helps maintain structure and supports active microbial communities. It acts as a store 1 405|for C while management practices support it, but change in practices can cause subsequent losses of 2 406|stored soil C. The balance of GHGE shows that despite the loss of C in soil storage with combustion, 3 407 4|this does not lead to a net increase in GHG emissions when the whole production system is taken 5 408|into account. We only considered a range of soils from England, where most turkey production is 6 409|found. The rate of decay of C depends on the clay content of soil and climate. The balance may thus 7 410|be different in other areas. The sensitivity analysis clearly showed the importance of the loss of soil 8 411|C. It should, however, be noted that the expected changes in the soil C content may differ depending 9 412 10|on the assumptions of the soil C model used in the analysis. The RothC soil C model, which was 413 11|applied in this study, has been extensively used across the world and compared with measured 12 414|changes in diverse ecosystems, e.g. Cerri et al. (2003). This reinforces our confidence in the other 13 415|main term addressing the net GHG emissions, namely the loss of soil carbon as a result of alternative 14 416|use of poultry litter. 15 16 417|One limitation of the use of poultry litter as a fertiliser is its high nutrient concentration. Providing a 17 418|large supply of C (to maintain the soil C storage) will be limited by the danger of an oversupply of N, 18 419 19|P and K. Indeed, the high N concentration in litter is one motive for using litter as a fuel. 20 420|The benefits for ammonia emissions are considerable when the litter is used as fuel. The current use 21 421|of turkey litter as a fuel saves about 0.9 kt ammonia emissions a year in the UK. This would be 22 422|increased by about a further 1.3 kt if litter use for fuel increase to 80% compared with the current 23 423 24|estimate of 35%. This would be about four times higher if applied also to broiler litter. 25 424|The scale of poultry farming in parts of eastern England has been an economic success for the 26 425 27|industry, but this has contributed to some environmental problems and, later, solutions. High 426 28|ammonia emissions from litter are one problem as well as eutrophication. The high concentrations 29 427|of poultry in parts of eastern England (plus other available biomass, like straw) also renders the 30 428|solution of fuel use an economic success, which also has environmental benefits. A more dispersed 31 429|poultry industry would lead to a less favourable structure for centralised fuel use, with increased 32 430|transport burdens. However, on-farm fuel use of litter has also become possible in Britain recently 33 431 34|through a change in the definition of litter in the waste management regulations. The main driver 35 432|behind on-farm use is to replace heating fuel with heat from litter. A broadly similar outcome would 36 433|occur if the small-scale fuel use of litter was analysed in the same way as with centralised power 37 434|stations. There would be different substitutions, e.g. fertiliser value for heating energy, and no extra 38 435 39|transport requirement, so relative magnitudes would differ. 40 436|Uncertainties of the estimated environmental impacts of UK turkey production were previously 41 437|quantified by Leinonen et al. (2015) but these are not reported quantitatively in this study. We 42 438 43|consider that there are too many areas in which the uncertainties of the model inputs are 439 44|unavailable to make a formal uncertainty analysis. However, we have high quality activity data, with 45 440|the litter collection and bird production data from the largest UK turkey producers, which accounts 46 441|for about 90% of housed UK turkey production. Litter use by power stations is recorded publicly and 47 442|used in other areas that require robust data, e.g. the UK GHG inventory. The turkey production 48 443|systems model is based on extensive data from the industry, including bird growth rates, diets and 49 444 50|resource use. The main input variable of the model, i.e. the amount of electricity generated per unit 445 51|of combusted litter was checked against other data sources. Quiroga et al. (2010) found that the 52 446|lower heating value of laying poultry manure was 12.1 MJ [kg DM] hence being 5.8 MJ [kg FW]¹ at 53 447|48% DM. Yassin et al. (2009) analysed fluidised-bed biomass combustion and found an energy 54 448|efficiency of 22%, giving at electricity output of 0.35 kWh electricity [kg FW]¹ 1 which is close to the 55 449 56|value given by ERPL of 0.31 kWh electricity [kg FW] This gives considerable confidence in the 450 57|analysis. 58 451|As discussed above, turkey litter was used as the basis of this analysis, because of the high quality 59 452 60|activity data available to us. CONCLUSIONS 4 5 456|As far as we are aware, this is the first study that systematically compares the environmental 6 457|impacts of using turkey litter either generate electricity instead or using turkey litter as fertilizer and 7 458 8|including turkey production. The results show that there are substantial environmental benefits 459 9|from using turkey litter as a fuel to rather than using it directly as a fertiliser. The main benefits are 10 460|from reduced ammonia emissions and saving primary fossil energy by generating electricity from 11 461|biomass. There is still a substantial input of fossil energy into the electricity produced resulting from 12 462|use in turkey production and litter collection. The very small decrease in greenhouse gas emissions 13 463|is the result of a fine balance between the loss of soil C and the displacement of electricity from a 14 464 15|marginal combined cycle gas turbine generator. There were, however, small increases in NMVOC, 16 465|NOx, dioxin, and PM- 10 emissions and such emissions must be managed and regulated in the most 17 466|stringent way to avoid any hazard to health. The increases in dioxins and PM 10 were not related to 18 467|combustion emissions, whereas those of NOx clearly were. 19 20 468|6. 36: 404|2005). Soil C helps maintain structure and supports active microbial communities. It acts as a store 1 405|for C while management practices support it, but change in practices can cause subsequent losses of 2 406|stored soil C. The balance of GHGE shows that despite the loss of C in soil storage with combustion, 3 407 4|this does not lead to a net increase in GHG emissions when the whole production system is taken 5 408|into account. We only considered a range of soils from England, where most turkey production is 6 409|found. The rate of decay of C depends on the clay content of soil and climate. The balance may thus 7 410|be different in other areas. The sensitivity analysis clearly showed the importance of the loss of soil 8 411|C. It should, however, be noted that the expected changes in the soil C content may differ depending 9 412 10|on the assumptions of the soil C model used in the analysis. The RothC soil C model, which was 413 11|applied in this study, has been extensively used across the world and compared with measured 12 414|changes in diverse ecosystems, e.g. Cerri et al. (2003). This reinforces our confidence in the other 13 415|main term addressing the net GHG emissions, namely the loss of soil carbon as a result of alternative 14 416|use of poultry litter. 15 16 417|One limitation of the use of poultry litter as a fertiliser is its high nutrient concentration. Providing a 17 418|large supply of C (to maintain the soil C storage) will be limited by the danger of an oversupply of N, 18 419 19|P and K. Indeed, the high N concentration in litter is one motive for using litter as a fuel. 20 420|The benefits for ammonia emissions are considerable when the litter is used as fuel. The current use 21 421|of turkey litter as a fuel saves about 0.9 kt ammonia emissions a year in the UK. This would be 22 422|increased by about a further 1.3 kt if litter use for fuel increase to 80% compared with the current 23 423 24|estimate of 35%. This would be about four times higher if applied also to broiler litter. 25 424|The scale of poultry farming in parts of eastern England has been an economic success for the 26 425 27|industry, but this has contributed to some environmental problems and, later, solutions. High 426 28|ammonia emissions from litter are one problem as well as eutrophication. The high concentrations 29 427|of poultry in parts of eastern England (plus other available biomass, like straw) also renders the 30 428|solution of fuel use an economic success, which also has environmental benefits. A more dispersed 31 429|poultry industry would lead to a less favourable structure for centralised fuel use, with increased 32 430|transport burdens. However, on-farm fuel use of litter has also become possible in Britain recently 33 431 34|through a change in the definition of litter in the waste management regulations. The main driver 35 432|behind on-farm use is to replace heating fuel with heat from litter. A broadly similar outcome would 36 433|occur if the small-scale fuel use of litter was analysed in the same way as with centralised power 37 434|stations. There would be different substitutions, e.g. fertiliser value for heating energy, and no extra 38 435 39|transport requirement, so relative magnitudes would differ. 40 436|Uncertainties of the estimated environmental impacts of UK turkey production were previously 41 437|quantified by Leinonen et al. (2015) but these are not reported quantitatively in this study. We 42 438 43|consider that there are too many areas in which the uncertainties of the model inputs are 439 44|unavailable to make a formal uncertainty analysis. However, we have high quality activity data, with 45 440|the litter collection and bird production data from the largest UK turkey producers, which accounts 46 441|for about 90% of housed UK turkey production. Litter use by power stations is recorded publicly and 47 442|used in other areas that require robust data, e.g. the UK GHG inventory. The turkey production 48 443|systems model is based on extensive data from the industry, including bird growth rates, diets and 49 444 50|resource use. The main input variable of the model, i.e. the amount of electricity generated per unit 445 51|of combusted litter was checked against other data sources. Quiroga et al. (2010) found that the 52 446|lower heating value of laying poultry manure was 12.1 MJ [kg DM] hence being 5.8 MJ [kg FW]¹ at 53 447|48% DM. Yassin et al. (2009) analysed fluidised-bed biomass combustion and found an energy 54 448|efficiency of 22%, giving at electricity output of 0.35 kWh electricity [kg FW]¹ 1 which is close to the 55 449 56|value given by ERPL of 0.31 kWh electricity [kg FW] This gives considerable confidence in the 450 57|analysis. 58 451|As discussed above, turkey litter was used as the basis of this analysis, because of the high quality 59 452 60|activity data available to us. CONCLUSIONS 4 5 456|As far as we are aware, this is the first study that systematically compares the environmental 6 457|impacts of using turkey litter either generate electricity instead or using turkey litter as fertilizer and 7 458 8|including turkey production. The results show that there are substantial environmental benefits 459 9|from using turkey litter as a fuel to rather than using it directly as a fertiliser. The main benefits are 10 460|from reduced ammonia emissions and saving primary fossil energy by generating electricity from 11 461|biomass. There is still a substantial input of fossil energy into the electricity produced resulting from 12 462|use in turkey production and litter collection. The very small decrease in greenhouse gas emissions 13 463|is the result of a fine balance between the loss of soil C and the displacement of electricity from a 14 464 15|marginal combined cycle gas turbine generator. There were, however, small increases in NMVOC, 16 465|NOx, dioxin, and PM- 10 emissions and such emissions must be managed and regulated in the most 17 466|stringent way to avoid any hazard to health. The increases in dioxins and PM 10 were not related to 18 467|combustion emissions, whereas those of NOx clearly were. 19 20 468|6. 37: 404|2005). Soil C helps maintain structure and supports active microbial communities. It acts as a store 1 405|for C while management practices support it, but change in practices can cause subsequent losses of 2 406|stored soil C. The balance of GHGE shows that despite the loss of C in soil storage with combustion, 3 407 4|this does not lead to a net increase in GHG emissions when the whole production system is taken 5 408|into account. We only considered a range of soils from England, where most turkey production is 6 409|found. The rate of decay of C depends on the clay content of soil and climate. The balance may thus 7 410|be different in other areas. The sensitivity analysis clearly showed the importance of the loss of soil 8 411|C. It should, however, be noted that the expected changes in the soil C content may differ depending 9 412 10|on the assumptions of the soil C model used in the analysis. The RothC soil C model, which was 413 11|applied in this study, has been extensively used across the world and compared with measured 12 414|changes in diverse ecosystems, e.g. Cerri et al. (2003). This reinforces our confidence in the other 13 415|main term addressing the net GHG emissions, namely the loss of soil carbon as a result of alternative 14 416|use of poultry litter. 15 16 417|One limitation of the use of poultry litter as a fertiliser is its high nutrient concentration. Providing a 17 418|large supply of C (to maintain the soil C storage) will be limited by the danger of an oversupply of N, 18 419 19|P and K. Indeed, the high N concentration in litter is one motive for using litter as a fuel. 20 420|The benefits for ammonia emissions are considerable when the litter is used as fuel. The current use 21 421|of turkey litter as a fuel saves about 0.9 kt ammonia emissions a year in the UK. This would be 22 422|increased by about a further 1.3 kt if litter use for fuel increase to 80% compared with the current 23 423 24|estimate of 35%. This would be about four times higher if applied also to broiler litter. 25 424|The scale of poultry farming in parts of eastern England has been an economic success for the 26 425 27|industry, but this has contributed to some environmental problems and, later, solutions. High 426 28|ammonia emissions from litter are one problem as well as eutrophication. The high concentrations 29 427|of poultry in parts of eastern England (plus other available biomass, like straw) also renders the 30 428|solution of fuel use an economic success, which also has environmental benefits. A more dispersed 31 429|poultry industry would lead to a less favourable structure for centralised fuel use, with increased 32 430|transport burdens. However, on-farm fuel use of litter has also become possible in Britain recently 33 431 34|through a change in the definition of litter in the waste management regulations. The main driver 35 432|behind on-farm use is to replace heating fuel with heat from litter. A broadly similar outcome would 36 433|occur if the small-scale fuel use of litter was analysed in the same way as with centralised power 37 434|stations. There would be different substitutions, e.g. fertiliser value for heating energy, and no extra 38 435 39|transport requirement, so relative magnitudes would differ. 40 436|Uncertainties of the estimated environmental impacts of UK turkey production were previously 41 437|quantified by Leinonen et al. (2015) but these are not reported quantitatively in this study. We 42 438 43|consider that there are too many areas in which the uncertainties of the model inputs are 439 44|unavailable to make a formal uncertainty analysis. However, we have high quality activity data, with 45 440|the litter collection and bird production data from the largest UK turkey producers, which accounts 46 441|for about 90% of housed UK turkey production. Litter use by power stations is recorded publicly and 47 442|used in other areas that require robust data, e.g. the UK GHG inventory. The turkey production 48 443|systems model is based on extensive data from the industry, including bird growth rates, diets and 49 444 50|resource use. The main input variable of the model, i.e. the amount of electricity generated per unit 445 51|of combusted litter was checked against other data sources. Quiroga et al. (2010) found that the 52 446|lower heating value of laying poultry manure was 12.1 MJ [kg DM] hence being 5.8 MJ [kg FW]¹ at 53 447|48% DM. Yassin et al. (2009) analysed fluidised-bed biomass combustion and found an energy 54 448|efficiency of 22%, giving at electricity output of 0.35 kWh electricity [kg FW]¹ 1 which is close to the 55 449 56|value given by ERPL of 0.31 kWh electricity [kg FW] This gives considerable confidence in the 450 57|analysis. 58 451|As discussed above, turkey litter was used as the basis of this analysis, because of the high quality 59 452 60|activity data available to us. CONCLUSIONS 4 5 456|As far as we are aware, this is the first study that systematically compares the environmental 6 457|impacts of using turkey litter either generate electricity instead or using turkey litter as fertilizer and 7 458 8|including turkey production. The results show that there are substantial environmental benefits 459 9|from using turkey litter as a fuel to rather than using it directly as a fertiliser. The main benefits are 10 460|from reduced ammonia emissions and saving primary fossil energy by generating electricity from 11 461|biomass. There is still a substantial input of fossil energy into the electricity produced resulting from 12 462|use in turkey production and litter collection. The very small decrease in greenhouse gas emissions 13 463|is the result of a fine balance between the loss of soil C and the displacement of electricity from a 14 464 15|marginal combined cycle gas turbine generator. There were, however, small increases in NMVOC, 16 465|NOx, dioxin, and PM- 10 emissions and such emissions must be managed and regulated in the most 17 466|stringent way to avoid any hazard to health. The increases in dioxins and PM 10 were not related to 18 467|combustion emissions, whereas those of NOx clearly were. 19 20 468|6. 38: <header>Appendix 1 Post generation losses from a CCGT </header>
The UK Digest of Energy Statistics (DUKES, 2015) describes losses in 2014 as 7.9% of energy demand. The losses comprise three components:

Yassin, L., Lettieri, P., Simons, S.J.R., Germanà, A. (2009) Techno-economic performance of energy from waste fluidized bed combustion and gasification processes in the UK context. Chemical Engineering Journal, 146 (3), 315-327

<list>Transmission losses (6.5 TWh) from the high voltage transmission system 
Theft or meter fraud (1.0 TWh) 
Distribution losses (21 TWh), which occur between the gateways to the public supply system's network and the customers' meters, and accounted for about 74 per cent of losses</list>

It was assumed losses applying here were distribution losses and that half occurred at the generation plant. The proportion of post generation losses (PGL) was estimated at: 21 X 0.5 PGL = 6.5 + 21 + 1

0: <title>Highlights for Environmental benefits of using turkey litter as a fuel instead of a fertiliser </title>

<list>Turkey litter handling by land application and electricity generation were compared 
Environmental life cycle assessment (LCA) was used as the method 
The main benefits were from a large reduction in ammonia (NH3) emissions 
Cumulative primary energy demand was reduced by 14% 
Loss of soil C reduced beneficial climate change impacts</list>
